posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Lisa Perrotti-Brown MW launches ‘fiercely independent’ wine publication

Lots of interesting details here, but this stood out:

… Speaking to db last week ahead of the official launch of the new publication, Perrotti-Brown said that she and [her 50-50 partner Swedish photojournalist Johan Berglund] “had been playing with so many names” for the title, but “it was The Wine Independent that came to us, because it seemed to say exactly what we wanted.”

Continuing, she told db, “I don’t want to point fingers at anyone else’s business in particular, but people don’t know what’s happening behind the scenes of so many wine criticism publications, unless you’re in the business, and then you are like, ‘oh my God’.”

She added, “A consumer reading a publication that tells you what you should and shouldn’t buy has no idea there are other agendas behind the scenes, so we are saying we are independent in the name, there is never going to be any wriggle room on that, there is never going to be any compromise on integrity, and that comes with us [Perotti-Brown and Berglund] being majority shareholders, and the others being very much in agreement with our ethos – no-one is going to get greedy; we want to remain completely unbiased.”

Indeed, in the press release on the title’s launch, which was sent to db last week, the new publication is said to “return to the high ethical standards initially championed by Robert M. Parker, Jr. back when he started The Wine Advocate in 1978,” noting that “a reputable wine criticism publication should not accept money from any other wine-related entities” …

It’s a fairly crowded market, with folks like Dunnuck and Suckling going solo with a subscription-based business model, plus Wine Advocate, Vinous, Jancis Robinson and Decanter.

It will be interesting to see if she focuses on particular regions. The article doesn’t really address that.

She seems to be implying that a couple of the things she disapproves of are clearly things that Galloni does. The 48 hour preview and the deals with producers for wine at his events. It seems though, when Parker sold the Advocate, there were plenty of events surrounding that too. I do not know the ins and outs of either deal.

Net though; I shall not be subscribing as her palate is in no way aligned with mine. I do wish her luck though, and hope that she puts the spotlight on many of the conflicts and other behind the scenes shenanigans critics get up to. It seems that in a field where the reader is the ultimate audience and payer, there should be no conflicts, unless very clearly defined.

The release also mentions the “coming to light” of “numerous conflicts of interest” at wine criticism publications – none of which are named – such as “selling event tables to wineries and score previews to retailers through ultra-premium subscriptions”.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
She seems to be implying that a couple of the things she disapproves of are clearly things that Galloni does. The 48 hour preview and the deals with producers for wine at his events. It seems though, when Parker sold the Advocate, there were plenty of events surrounding that too. I do not know the ins and outs of either deal.

She might also be referring to her own experience. Parker hired her as a critic around 2008, and then the Singapore investors who bought control of the Wine Advocate from Parker in 2012 appointed her editor in chief. They were in the wine business and their business model seemed to be to monetize the brand via events in Asia with the help of the trade.

Michelin, which bought 40% in 2017 then bought the rest in 2019, has a long reputation for independent restaurant and hotel ratings, but I don’t know how that’s played out for WA.

[Corrected: Originally I had written that Parker appointed her editor in chief. - John]

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
Lisa Perrotti-Brown MW launches ‘fiercely independent’ wine publication

Lots of interesting details here, but this stood out:

… Speaking to db last week ahead of the official launch of the new publication, Perrotti-Brown said that she and [her 50-50 partner Swedish photojournalist Johan Berglund] “had been playing with so many names” for the title, but “it was The Wine Independent that came to us, because it seemed to say exactly what we wanted.”

Continuing, she told db, “I don’t want to point fingers at anyone else’s business in particular, but people don’t know what’s happening behind the scenes of so many wine criticism publications, unless you’re in the business, and then you are like, ‘oh my God’.”

She added, “A consumer reading a publication that tells you what you should and shouldn’t buy has no idea there are other agendas behind the scenes, so we are saying we are independent in the name, there is never going to be any wriggle room on that, there is never going to be any compromise on integrity, and that comes with us [Perotti-Brown and Berglund] being majority shareholders, and the others being very much in agreement with our ethos – no-one is going to get greedy; we want to remain completely unbiased.”

Indeed, in the press release on the title’s launch, which was sent to db last week, the new publication is said to “return to the high ethical standards initially championed by Robert M. Parker, Jr. back when he started The Wine Advocate in 1978,” noting that “a reputable wine criticism publication should not accept money from any other wine-related entities” …

Truth is though that anyone who reads the reams of over-the-top praise and uniform 95+ scores given to established expensive wines can tell that critics have little freedom to be critical or even unenthusiastic in reviewing the major producers. There are a few exceptions (Jancis?) but that’s the rule. Thing is, she was very much one of the crowd in that respect so it makes me doubt her ability to challenge it.

But in the age of CT endless lookalike tasting notes for in-barrel wines are not what I look for from wine writers anyway. I want good wine writing that takes me inside the winemaking practices, viticulture, culture, history, geography, economics etc. of a region

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Wow! Quite the statement!!

Thanks for sharing!

I get the sense LPB has always viewed herself as the spiritual as well as actual successor to Parker, who always positioned himself in this manner (a protector of consumers first). She was fiercely protective of his legacy at The Wine Advocate whereas I think the current reviewers/editors are less focused on that legacy and more focused on their style of reviewing and reporting. So I’m not really surprised she has started this new publication with a cri de couer in this style.

posts.name: P eter L owet
posts.cooked:
Subscriber-only model makes sense in today’s world assuming she has the name recognition or can benefit off of her experience at WA.

Actually, I’m worried that the subscriber-only model is NOT economically feasible. It seems like the best known reviewer “publications” are profitable only by monetizing their influencer profile with events, merchandising, ads, and/or “special access”. All of the side revenue creates potential conflicts of interest. I don’t see how a new venture will viably overcome this barrier to “independence”.

Given the inherent conflicts, a subscriber can’t rely solely on editorial rules/regulations; one needs to believe in a reviewer’s integrity. It’s too easy otherwise to throw unsubstantiated accusations. (See recent threads on 2019 Napa and 2020 smoke taint. Fine to disagree, but why did so many posters question particular reviewers’ motives? I suspect that most people throwing innuendo do not subscribe to the targeted publication.)

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
What is wrong with a wine rating publication hosting a wine tasting?

Admittedly, I never attended those that are being discussed but I would find being able to attend and compare my palate side by side with a published reviewer to be extremely helpful and fun. I guess I could see where it could become too commercialized, but on its face doesn’t seem to be an indicator of a reviewers lack of independence.

I think the issue is that in reality, the events would end up looking like Wine Spectator style events where producers have to contribute their time, expertise, and wines – all gratis – and the attendees pay a fee, while the promoter collects most of the monies from the event. So it morphs into a tradeshow.

Is this a game the small artisanal farmer can play? or is this something that the big brands and their publicists will be better at?

========

I do think having even more reviewers, especially “newer” ones desperate for media/retailer attention, just leads to further score inflation. Slapping a high score on something pretty much means some retailers will start using those to sell their wares, and licensing fees will start to flow in. If LPB’s scores are more tempered, the reviews won’t move merchandise, and the new venture will not get any trade subscribers.

In any case, as Keith mentions upthread, I look forward to the 9th set of reviews on Latour en barrel, a worthless exercise for the reviewers, and the readers. Maybe we’ll get a few more citations of ‘singed alder’; I’ve been missing that.

Exactly.

It’s hard to ask the producers to participate and contribute wines if you don’t give them good scores.

The incentive to inflate scores also works indirectly: The more your publication is used in shelf-talkers and quoted in retailers’ emails (because you gave an astronomical score and gushed about the wine), the better known your critic’s brand becomes with consumers. That, in turn, allows you to monetize it through events or advertising or whatever.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
(responding to Neal) Yes, but there were certainly wines Parker purchased (in bottle) and would review, long after he had been PNG’d by the estate themselves. Old school St Emilion Soutard is one where this played out over many years, even though the wine would actually receive good reviews.

=======

Every business model is going to come with attendant risks anyone who is interacting with them should be aware of. Just to be Devil’s Advocate, another way of thinking of independent reviews is that they are unaccountable, with limited stake in their views. An importer who has to commit to a pallet or a shipping container of wine may have a more gimlet eye on the quality / value of what they are sending 3000-6000 miles. Are the views independent, disinterested any more? No, but are they careless, ill thought out? unlikely. If I see KLWM back strips on a Rhone or Laguedoc/Roussillon that’s a pretty strong endorsement: a review backed with money per se.

All the craziness going among paid critics basically means that nowadays, that I don’t pay for any of them. I pay for WB, underground storage, and recieve a free sub to WS (so I can keep up with Jon Bon Jovi’s newest Hampton Water branded sneaker side gig along with what pro atheletes are drinking e.g. Opus One by the case for one NFL’er). There’s enough wine books by my armchair to keep me occupied for another six months; although I suppose to be fair I likely spend $100-200 a year on books in that niche.

Running ads doesn’t necessarily make you biased. Newspapers run ads from Hollywood and review movies. Car publications run car ads and review cars. Music publications run music industry ads and review albums and concerts. And so on.

At a casual glance, I don’t think the wineries and conglomerates that run ads in WS tend to get high scores, or that many of the high scoring wines run ads there. posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
In the gray area are free samples. In the early years, I believe that Parker said he bought all the wines he reviewed. I think that policy was abandoned long, long ago, though.

How are free samples a gray area?

Barrel samples have no value. And bottled samples have zero value once they’re opened. 99.9% of all the samples sent are given away or poured out at the end of the day. Perhaps a few bottles are shared over dinner. But after a long day of tasting, the last thing most reviewers want to do is drink wine.

Samples are given in exchange for reviews. It’s a trade.

So where is the gray area?

Any reviewer or publication tasting thousands or tens of thousands of wines each year could never earn enough money to pay for all the samples they taste, especially at todays prices.

There are potential gray areas but that’s not one of them.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
It’s been very interesting to read the different takes. Look forward to reading more. This place has always been fun to watch since its inception.

We thought it important to state which business model we are setting out to build. To mention what we will be steering away from and why we think it’s important to do so, seems natural.

It’s becoming an increasingly competitive field and each must find their way of creating revenue in order to publish the very best content they can. Not an easy thing to do. Luckily for us, we have a very enviable and low overhead cost, by quite a long margin in comparison. This allows us a break-even point which others in this niche industry would call not just impossible but down right hay feverish. It all means we can be comfortable in our mission as declared.

In the end the only thing we can do, like all others, is try and produce interesting enough content that people will subscribe. Some will trust us, some will not. But we will stay true to our beliefs, such as they are. It’s a very exciting thing to be at the beginning of a journey, knowing that you yourself are steering the ship. We will grow slowly and not get ahead of ourselves. This is our only shot at this.

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
Samples are given in exchange for reviews. It’s a trade.

So where is the gray area?

Seems clear to me: If you give bad reviews, you might not get free samples. This could impact your ability to function as a wine critic.

(Not saying I think your reviews are compromised in any way, just answering your question.)

A friend of mine is a car journalist. He gets to test drive new models of less and more expensive cars. Sometimes he has them for the weekend. Sometimes he has them for months (long term reviews). There are also events organized at racetracks or various venues to compare cars in the same categories. Most (almost all) of it is paid by the car manufacturers and or dealerships.
Car manufacturers also place ads in the online and paper publications he works for. Same questionable model.

He’s honest as can be. He tells it how it is (or at least how he perceives it). He understands that’s the required approach. And I think the car manufacturers and dealers respect him for that. He drives a Subaru with a few years on it.

I think the business model is what the business model is. You need to find reviewers you trust and trust their integrity. And I’m normally pretty skeptical and cynical.

The danger is not that the producer or distributor will be stocking the critic’s cellar.* It’s that the critic may be aware that he/she will have to pay for samples in the future if they are highly critical of the wine in print.

* In fact, some importers sent duplicates of each wine in case one was defective. In that case, they may well have been stocking the critic’s cellar!

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
I am surprised that everyone seems to think this is a call out of a single critic or publication as being overly influenced in their decisions. I always kinda figured it was taken for granted that they all have ads in their publications that probably wouldn’t be there to advertise scores in the 80s, or have top 100 lists that have mid 90s scoring wines on them for a reason, or 100 other things that make this whole group more than a little biased. am I reading this correctly to assume that people think galoni is somehow more bought than suckling? or (insert name of other critic thats not William kelley here)

Running ads doesn’t necessarily make you biased. Newspapers run ads from Hollywood and review movies. Car publications run car ads and review cars. Music publications run music industry ads and review albums and concerts. And so on.

At a casual glance, I don’t think the wineries and conglomerates that run ads in WS tend to get high scores, or that many of the high scoring wines run ads there. Between that and the complete lack of any evidence that they’re selling scores for ad dollars, I think the correct presumption is that they aren’t doing anything wrong. But I’m old fashioned about stuff like that.

The risk of conflicts between independence/integrity and advertisers is much higher with trade publications such as wine journals whose ad revenue comes entirely from one industry. They may be reliant on subscriber income from their industry, too. It’s a pervasive problem with trade publications. Some may have enough independent subscription income that will not disappear with critical articles. But others (e.g., Wine Spectator) are pretty much given away and rely mostly and ad income.

Newspapers and general interest news websites have much more diversified ad bases, so they aren’t so vulnerable to pressure from a single, or a few, advertisers, and can afford to be more critical without fear of jeopardizing their businesses. In the case of major news sources, they typically derive a high proportion of their income from subscriptions.

I don’t see a real issue with free samples provided for tasting. It is true that a producer who gets a bad review might refuse to provide samples thereafter, and in fact I know this has happened on a few occasions. Parker was famously unwelcome at a number of important Burgundy addresses for years, so they didn’t get Parker reviews (a solution that worked out fine for both parties, I guess). I also recall that there were a few wines he continued to review sporadically after being made persona non grata by buying his own bottles.

In any event, the freebie of samples for tasting doesn’t seem to be of sufficient magnitude to curry the sort of favor many seem to think it does. Just my opinion. Splashy display ads are another thing, especially for a business trying to survive in a dying industry.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
In the gray area are free samples. In the early years, I believe that Parker said he bought all the wines he reviewed. I think that policy was abandoned long, long ago, though.

How are free samples a gray area?

Barrel samples have no value. And bottled samples have zero value once they’re opened. 99.9% of all the samples sent are given away or poured out at the end of the day. Perhaps a few bottles are shared over dinner. But after a long day of tasting, the last thing most reviewers want to do is drink wine.

Samples are given in exchange for reviews. It’s a trade.

So where is the gray area?

Any reviewer or publication tasting thousands or tens of thousands of wines each year could never earn enough money to pay for all the samples they taste, especially at todays prices.

There are potential gray areas but that’s not one of them.

Love ya, Jeff, but that’s close to the most obtuse thing that I have seen you write. It’s a pure quid pro quo, and by any definition of a conflict of interest, this would be it, if the reviewer is holding himself or herself out as being independent. It’s the exact opposite; it is dependent. You being invited to the greatest Chateau for barrel samples, and being sent hundreds of bottles for tasting - gratis - has huge value that most of us will never experience and could not afford to replicate. And you get to do this year after year. As a consequence of all of these freebies, critics appear to make money off their commercial venue. Nobody would subscribe if they did not review all of these free wines.

We are big boys and girls. We know the drill here. But let’s not call it something that it is not. Or act is if the relationship is not cozy. It’s up to use to weigh that in the context of what and who we value.

I read your site often and value your contributions, so I’m not bashing here, just calling it what it is.

posts.name: Jeff Leve

Seems clear to me: If you give bad reviews, you might not get free samples. This could impact your ability to function as a wine critic.

That doesn’t happen often, very few producers have taken that stance. While they all my want good notes/scores, they are fine with honest, thoughtful notes because there’s always another vintage and more wine every year.

Also, the entire universe of wine is massive. Most wines are not that good. The majority of reviews are usually for the better wines being made, so most scores are not going to be for plonk. When you add that most reviews don’t publish below 85, it’s difficult to find notes slamming wines.

There are pricey wines that do not always get high marks from everyone. Look at the thread on Gallonis 2019 Napa coverage with scores on the 80s.

The reality is most producers will not stop letting you taste if you’re honest and polite about your views.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
We believe in being transparent about how we generate revenue. We do that because we think that as a consumer you should be able to decide which voice you want to follow. Which business you are comfortable with supporting. Without transparency that is not possible.

All we’re doing is attempting to explain why we chose our name and how we feel we differ. We’re just talking about actual realities. None of which are actually secret. Just not brought into the light quite so often. But when they are, then it is frequently right here on this forum.

If the things we talk about are important to a consumer, then that consumer is free to make a choice. If we stay quiet, then the same consumer has less information on which to base their choice.

To me this board is a perfect example of how transparency should be paramount, rather than there being subjects not allowed to mention because of seeming to be a person of poor taste. It is why I appreciate it.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
It’s been very interesting to read the different takes. Look forward to reading more. This place has always been fun to watch since its inception.

We thought it important to state which business model we are setting out to build. To mention what we will be steering away from and why we think it’s important to do so, seems natural.

It’s becoming an increasingly competitive field and each must find their way of creating revenue in order to publish the very best content they can. Not an easy thing to do. Luckily for us, we have a very enviable and low overhead cost, by quite a long margin in comparison. This allows us a break-even point which others in this niche industry would call not just impossible but down right hay feverish. It all means we can be comfortable in our mission as declared.

In the end the only thing we can do, like all others, is try and produce interesting enough content that people will subscribe. Some will trust us, some will not. But we will stay true to our beliefs, such as they are. It’s a very exciting thing to be at the beginning of a journey, knowing that you yourself are steering the ship. We will grow slowly and not get ahead of ourselves. This is our only shot at this.

Johan,

Thanks for jumping on here - it means a great deal to all of us.

One quick question - will there be a ‘winery’ or ‘retailer’ membership option as well?

Cheers.

Transparency.

Ive lost count of how many times Ive seen this scenario play out in just about every industry. Maybe i’m wrong. Maybe somewhere out there are there are hundreds of thousands of wine drinkers wishing if only I could get more neutral wine reviews. Id love to plunk down $5 a month for a lifetime subscription. Or maybe not. The current succesful business models are what they are for a reason. They evolve based upon market demand to survive

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
We believe in being transparent about how we generate revenue. We do that because we think that as a consumer you should be able to decide which voice you want to follow. Which business you are comfortable with supporting. Without transparency that is not possible.

All we’re doing is attempting to explain why we chose our name and how we feel we differ. We’re just talking about actual realities. None of which are actually secret. Just not brought into the light quite so often. But when they are, then it is frequently right here on this forum.

If the things we talk about are important to a consumer, then that consumer is free to make a choice. If we stay quiet, then the same consumer has less information on which to base their choice.

To me this board is a perfect example of how transparency should be paramount, rather than there being subjects not allowed to mention because of seeming to be a person of poor taste. It is why I appreciate it.

Appreciate you taking the time to come on here and respond.

I have this question of sorts that I wanted to pose.

I feel like you are definitely appealing to a jaded client (maybe not the wording you would use), one that has doubts about critics, and you are trying to be the neutral voice in the marketplace, kind of like John Gilman. Do you think you guys are doing this a little late in the game? I feel like those potential clients gave up on wine critics a long time ago, and went their own route.

Is there a non-review portion of the business model as well?

Thanks Ian,

I believe there is actually a growing market and demand for a more independent voice. The late in the game feeling is most likely stemming from, and correct me if I’m wrong, having been around a while and seen things come and go?

The page views, search and subscriptions have all grown during Covid. There is a brave new consumer base out there and they have money they want to spend wisely.

We have identified what we believe are key revenue streams and markets which are currently not being hunted by the competition either because they are too small for them to bother about or they simply do not realize they exist. As stated earlier, we have a very different operation than most others and can find value where others can not.

I myself could also have been jaded after all these years in this business, but I have never been more excited about the future!

To your last point, yes, there will absolutely be a non-review section. Headed up by yours truly. Everyone needs a playground and I’ve gotten myself a pretty fancy one.

Cheers

[/quote]
It would be better for the consumer if these conflicts of interest didn’t exist. But they do and should be disclosed so consumers have that information when deciding which publications to subscribe.
[/quote]

Yup

WS invites suspicion and distrust by publishing ads from wineries whose wines it reviews. WS creates doubt by its actions; it is reasonable to be skeptical of WS’ advertising philosophy/practice and conclude that WS isn’t as obligated its subscribers as much as it is to its advertisers. I consider WS’ acceptance of ads to be disqualifying, while realizing that others may come to a different conclusion.

Mark, does that mean you do not trust any media that takes advertising, print, television, radio, online, etc.?

Hmmmmm… I agree w/ Jeff & Sao. The WS has repeatedly asserted that the presence of ads in the publication does not influence
the scores of the wines they review. I have no reason not to believe that claim & have not seen any evidence that it is not true.
I fully believe the tastings are done blind & the presence of ads cannot influence the scores they award.
That said, I have heard tell from a number of winemakers that they are given a head’s up that in an upcoming review, they have
a wine that scored well. And that, maybe…hint/hint…you might like to take out an ad in that coming issue. Or you may want to
pay extra to have a pic of your winemaker accompaning that said review.
However, in the case of awarding their Restaurant Awards of Excellence, I’m not convinced the presence of ads doesn’t influence
those awards.

For me, it’s all so easy. These wine reviews all have virtually no influence on my buying decisions. I cannot think of a single review,
in recent yrs, that compelled me to go out & buy that wine. And I have yet to figure out what these numbers they attach to a wine mean.
That was not so in the early days of Charlie/Earl’s CGtCW…wwhen it was a worthwhile publication. Often they would discover a wine/wnry
of which I’d never heard of, go out & buy the wine to try it. Oftentimes, their discovery became one of my favorite wineries. But that was in a day
long ago & a galaxy far/far away.
Tom

posts.name: Brian Befano
posts.cooked:
Samples are given in exchange for reviews. It’s a trade.

So where is the gray area?

Seems clear to me: If you give bad reviews, you might not get free samples. This could impact your ability to function as a wine critic.

(Not saying I think your reviews are compromised in any way, just answering your question.)

A friend of mine is a car journalist. He gets to test drive new models of less and more expensive cars. Sometimes he has them for the weekend. Sometimes he has them for months (long term reviews). There are also events organized at racetracks or various venues to compare cars in the same categories. Most (almost all) of it is paid by the car manufacturers and or dealerships.
Car manufacturers also place ads in the online and paper publications he works for. Same questionable model.

He’s honest as can be. He tells it how it is (or at least how he perceives it). He understands that’s the required approach. And I think the car manufacturers and dealers respect him for that. He drives a Subaru with a few years on it.

I think the business model is what the business model is. You need to find reviewers you trust and trust their integrity. And I’m normally pretty skeptical and cynical.

I really like the analogy and I think it raises a clear solution, blind tastings. Car reviewers can’t do the review blind, so some bias may be baked into the review, but wine reviews can, and I believe should, be done blind. If you really want to claim you are free of bias then eliminate all extraneous information and just taste the wine.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
I think the notion that reviewers inflate scores because if they don’t, a few producers might stop sending free samples for review, is a pretty big reach. It feels like a conclusion in search of a justification. My thinking:

(1) I doubt it happens more than rarely,

(2) there are an almost unlimited number of wines out there to review and if a few don’t get reviewed for this reason, who really notices or cares, and

(3) if the reviewer has given low scores for a number of years and then stops doing reviews of that producer, most people can fill in the blanks and there is no big loss of information to the reader.

And again, all kinds of products get reviewed based off free samples and trials. If that’s a disqualifier to you, you’ll have to forgo most reviews out there.

posts.cooked:

Typically, critics don’t get samples directly from the producer. It’s usually through an importer or distributor, so the critic may be dependent on that company’s cooperation for a long list of wines.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked:
There have been a few slightly odd suggestions here, not least that critics should pay for samples. So, basically you need to be extremely wealthy to start with to taste at DRC or Cheval? Kind of narrows your base, doesn’t it?

It’s not that I think they should turn down the free samples. It’s just that they have to decide what kind of wine writers they want to be. If it’s the kind of writing that depends on free samples, so be it, but then don’t define yourself according to notions of total independence, conflict-free consumer advocacy, etc. Alternatively, if total independence, no-conflict reporting is the focus, then review what the business can pay for. This isn’t a crazy suggestion. I’m pretty sure that’s what most of the major newspapers require of their wine columnists. Certainly they pay the checks for their restaurant reviews rather than asking for comped meals in exchange for press.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
So if you attend a UGC tasting, should you ask to be billed? And if the chateau does not want to be included in the large tastings, do you pay for each one individually and do you tip the cellar master? How about a trade tasting; do you have to bring a check?

It becomes absurd. There are a few areas where it is mutually advantageous to both the estate and the critic to have their wines tasted. If you accept that, then everything else becomes a decision as to what is acceptable within your own moral precepts.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
What is wrong with a wine rating publication hosting a wine tasting?

Admittedly, I never attended those that are being discussed but I would find being able to attend and compare my palate side by side with a published reviewer to be extremely helpful and fun. I guess I could see where it could become too commercialized, but on its face doesn’t seem to be an indicator of a reviewers lack of independence.

I think the issue is that in reality, the events would end up looking like Wine Spectator style events where producers have to contribute their time, expertise, and wines – all gratis – and the attendees pay a fee, while the promoter collects most of the monies from the event. So it morphs into a tradeshow.

Is this a game the small artisanal farmer can play? or is this something that the big brands and their publicists will be better at?

Exactly.

It’s hard to ask the producers to participate and contribute wines if you don’t give them good scores.

The incentive to inflate scores also works indirectly: The more your publication is used in shelf-talkers and quoted in retailers’ emails (because you gave an astronomical score and gushed about the wine), the better known your critic’s brand becomes with consumers. That, in turn, allows you to monetize it through events or advertising or whatever.

For sure. No one is submitting samples to get 88 points for a $50 wine even if it is an 88 point wine which there are plenty out there for that price.

posts.name: Cris Whetstone
posts.cooked:
A free sample of a bottle isn’t that big of a deal to me. I suppose that you might raise half an eyebrow at the free samples from high dollar, famous houses but if said reviewer provides a review from that bottle then so be it. There is some sort of treadmill effect here of having to keep everyone up to date year over year. And lets not forget that over some number of wines per year, this actually does turn into work. Not that we should feel sorry for reviewers. Only that its hard to look at 1000 bottles of wine as a gift if every one of them is to be opened, sampled and reviewed by one person in some short time frame.

If the ‘free sample’ is some multiple of bottles from one house per vintage, then that would be where there is some issue. But one bottle per seems hardly like a payoff for a good score.

I suppose that if some wine consumer coalition wanted to form and demand wineries only send sample sizes to critics then you could squash all this supposed impropriety.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
Consumers might not get samples sent, but they can go to the same chateau and taste most of the same wines during their visit as well.

There are things critics do that have the appearance of potential conflicts of interest. Samples are not one of them.

The difference is that nearly always in Bordeaux (since I think that’s the main point here) normal punters pay for their samples as part of the price of admission and it isn’t cheap.

I know some, if not most charge, but I do not think they all charge, especially at the top end. The First Growths, Petrus, Cheval etc. Though I am not sure who easy it is to go.

Clearly the chateau owner is not going to invite someone who doesn’t like their wines and if any of us has an epic dinner in charming company, are we really going to be objective afterwards?

Funny story that is 100% true. There was a high-level wine I was not fond of. The wines never got big scores from me. Just not my style. The director sent me an Email inviting me to lunch and an extensive vertical. I accepted as we had never met. We got along great! None of the wines in the vertical scored well. But within a few years, they upped their game and they are now making very good wine. Because the wines are better, they get better scores, at least from me, not because they had me for lunch. So, it is not always true. Scores are personal. But there is always another vintage on the way, so few owners/directors hold grudges.

I would hope a first growth would not pay. I think free samples are surely enough.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
For sure. No one is submitting samples to get 88 points for a $50 wine even if it is an 88 point wine which there are plenty out there for that price.

In the real world, though, most producers make many different wines, and then make all or most of them each vintage, so it doesn’t usually just work out like one $50 wine getting 88 year after year after year until the winery decides they’re better off not submitting samples.

I don’t have any critic subscriptions anymore to be able to provide actual examples, but I imagine if you look at Mondavi, Concha y Toro, Antinori or something, you’ll see lots of different wines with different scores, which then vary from one year to the next. Some of their $50 wines might get 88 or lower, but they will keep sending their portfolio in for reviews.

So reading through this, the main question seems to come back to Robert’s earliest question.

Does she have a following?

Parker came at a different time. Can’t use his model any more.

Some people, like Jeb, may be able to do it as a solo act, figuring that they need X number of subscribers at $x price per year and they could make a decent enough living, at least enough to pay for travel and a home. I don’t know Jeb’s model, but I assume it had to start something like that.

Galloni has a lot more overhead. Wine Spectator had even more - big office in Manhattan, administrative and clerical staff, a big payroll, etc. So they need to sell more than subscriptions, since a subscription model doesn’t seem to be working well these days. They only want subscribers so they can sell the subscribers’ eyeballs, which is their real money. And consequently their subscriptions are cheap.

Suckling is the smartest by far IMO - he sells wine tasting with himself as the ringmaster. His subscribers turn out for his events. To get them, he has to appeal to the widest audience.

Does the world need another wine reviewing publication? Nope.

But if it’s going to have one, the publication should be able to differentiate itself somehow. And in this case, unfortunately, I think it’s a misfire. You don’t start out by implying shady practices elsewhere. And you know you will never reveal any of those “shady” practices because the juiciest ones would result in a libel suit. So you have to imply, suggest, hint, etc.

It’s like you’re caught between Black Forest cake and a cast iron pan!

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
Suckling is the smartest by far IMO - he sells wine tasting with himself as the ringmaster. His subscribers turn out for his events. To get them, he has to appeal to the widest audience.

He seems to do a ton of events but wonder how that is going currently. Revenue certainly is down the last 2 years. I actually saw a Suckling 88 on a sparkling wine recently.

posts.name: Freelon F Hunter
posts.cooked:
It’s been very interesting to read the different takes. Look forward to reading more. This place has always been fun to watch since its inception.

We thought it important to state which business model we are setting out to build. To mention what we will be steering away from and why we think it’s important to do so, seems natural.

It’s becoming an increasingly competitive field and each must find their way of creating revenue in order to publish the very best content they can. Not an easy thing to do. Luckily for us, we have a very enviable and low overhead cost, by quite a long margin in comparison. This allows us a break-even point which others in this niche industry would call not just impossible but down right hay feverish. It all means we can be comfortable in our mission as declared.

In the end the only thing we can do, like all others, is try and produce interesting enough content that people will subscribe. Some will trust us, some will not. But we will stay true to our beliefs, such as they are. It’s a very exciting thing to be at the beginning of a journey, knowing that you yourself are steering the ship. We will grow slowly and not get ahead of ourselves. This is our only shot at this.

Johan, your story-telling role sounds intriguing. Like others, I really enjoyed your contribution to Neal Martin’s Pomerol. Will try out the site just to see what you create, I think!

Do you think you guys are doing this a little late in the game? I feel like those potential clients gave up on wine critics a long time ago, and went their own route.

Is there a non-review portion of the business model as well?

Thanks Ian,

I believe there is actually a growing market and demand for a more independent voice. The late in the game feeling is most likely stemming from, and correct me if I’m wrong, having been around a while and seen things come and go?

The page views, search and subscriptions have all grown during Covid. There is a brave new consumer base out there and they have money they want to spend wisely.

We have identified what we believe are key revenue streams and markets which are currently not being hunted by the competition either because they are too small for them to bother about or they simply do not realize they exist. As stated earlier, we have a very different operation than most others and can find value where others can not.

I myself could also have been jaded after all these years in this business, but I have never been more excited about the future!

To your last point, yes, there will absolutely be a non-review section. Headed up by yours truly. Everyone needs a playground and I’ve gotten myself a pretty fancy one.

Cheers

As one of the owners I think this opinion is the most important in this thread. Only time will tell if they were correct in their assessment of the market.

Thanks Ken,

It’s been hard work up until this point, and now the actual climb starts…

Cheers,

posts.name: CF Binnig
posts.cooked:
John didn’t mention this part because he was probably too giddy with excitement, but it appears that if you like Black Forest Cake in your wine, you’ll be able to find all the wines with Black Forest Cake:

That prospect does indeed make me giddy! But, sadly, I doubt she negotiated a deal going in to keep the rights to her own tasting notes, as Galloni did with Parker. So, I’d guess her database will only cover notes going forward, and she seems to have eschewed that term in the last year or so. Can’t imagine why.

We took the guillotine to that cake reference!

I will say, in LPB’s defense, that many of us know what Black Forest Cake tastes like. As opposed to, say, rusty nails, forest floor, lichen, tar, slate, petrol, sandalwood, gravel (these last two used just today by a noted winemaker to describe the flavors of one of his wines) or numerous other flavor descriptors that I have not tasted and have no desire to taste.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
So reading through this, the main question seems to come back to Robert’s earliest question.

Does she have a following?

Some people, like Jeb, may be able to do it as a solo act, figuring that they need X number of subscribers at $x price per year and they could make a decent enough living, at least enough to pay for travel and a home. I don’t know Jeb’s model, but I assume it had to start something like that.

Surprisingly, Jeb literally discusses his review business model, both when he initially started, and now, here

https://xchateau.com/episodes/engineering-wine-criticism-w-jeb-dunnuck-jebdunnuckcom-vw5Zf2Wm

I don’t think LPB has a (monetizable) following, and if anything, my general impression over the years is actually negative.

Hi Arv,

I think Jeb is on to something and we share many of those beliefs. He is doing well for a reason. Same goes for Jane.

As someone who spent time with close to 200 potential investors in searching for the small group I finally chose, I can tell you of some interesting observations, such as they are. You are free to disregard them if you want. I myself bet my literal farm on them.

I only talked to people who were outside of the wine world. All happy amateurs with money to spare. Many young(ish). Some small scale, some very large. The final group all have 1st growth cellars and quite sharp acumen when it comes to business, being hugely successful entrepreneurs in their respective fields. It was very much Dragons Den, or Shark Tank if you’re of that persuasion.

Amongst these people Robert Parker was obviously a legend. Outside of him there was one name which was more widely known than any other. Yes, it was Lisa Perrotti-Brown. When they did know another, it was Neal Martin. Any other name or publication was in the single digit percentile. In fact, Cellar Tracker had greater traction than other publications. Lisa was the key to The Wine Independent.

While I think this forum is a great place for listening to opinions and forming ideas, it is also a finite world and a bubble. The fame of critics is not a constant throughout all subsets of consumers.

This is the bottom line of what I pitched our investors: We see a bigger pie than our competition. We do not need to chase the revenue which would cause us to desert the core business of staying independent. You will have a minor stake. The critics will have all control and majority.

They did the math. Many wanted to join. I picked the ones who were most laid back.

As stated previously, now the real work begins. I could not be more excited. Which is probably just as well because I’m not going to get much sleep.

Cheers,

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
Does the world need another wine reviewing publication? Nope.

Hi Greg,

Quite a philosophical and interesting question that one.

I think the world is ever evolving and there will always be newcomers in all fields, wine or what have you, constantly cropping up wether the marketplace is saturated or not (depending on one’s view).

Does the world need another social media app with all that is already out there? Many will say no. Yet, the only thing certain is that it will come. Who will flock to it? At first, previously untapped customers and early adopters and people just in search of something new and shiny. After that, some people will migrate from previous apps. Some apps will fail and some will succeed.

And repeat.

The Wine Advocate was not the first wine publication and The Wine Independent certainly will not be the last. I know more than a few critics who are champing at the bit to strike out on their own. It will happen.

What can I say, Lisa and I are following an idealistic dream as our moral compasses both point north in the same direction. We have some strong beliefs and we’re willing to stand up to defend them. We are not implying anything. Just trying to widen the audience of the very discussions which can be found in many a thread on this forum.

Cheers,

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I think when you participate on this web site, there is a significant number of old codgers, such as myself, with old style palates, who like neither the recently made more glossy, alcoholic wines being made today or the fact that most critics score so many at or close to three figures.

We are not the majority. The wine world has changed and the paradigm has changed. Many here pursue our Magdelaines and Clos Rougeards praying we will find more traditional wines we can love. But we are being passed by and the critics don’t want to or can’t cater to our palates
Yes there is plenty of room for Lisa, as she represents the current target audience.

We are an interesting Luddite sub group alongside people who will value her review. The Luddites though are more vocal, they have something to defend.

posts.name: Chris CrutchfieId
posts.cooked:
For a place with so many people that rant and rave about disregarding critics scores and reviews there sure are a lot of people that care about said critics business model.

This is the intellectual equivalent of “Hmm you are critical of the current state of politics, yet you vote, how hypocritical.”

There is nothing at all conflicting about holding the opinion that:
a) Wine critics have an outsized influence on the world of wine, and ultimately how wines are priced on the marketplace.
b) Apparent conflicts of interest in wine critics’ business models lead one to question which master they serve.

Whether or not one individually pays attention to wine critics is immaterial, because their influence affects everybody who purchases wine.

Also, kinda sad how so many just automatically assume something untoward or conspiratorial is going on with wine critics with little or zero evidence of such. Representative of our society’s erosion in trust of fellow man I guess.

It doesn’t exactly take much brainpower to examine what we publicly know about wine critics’ business relationships and revenue models and conclude that there exist conflicts of interest. I don’t know about you, but trust is something that should be earned, not granted reflexively just because somebody is in a position of authority. Part of earning that trust ought to be doing everything conceivably possible to avoid the appearance of a conflict of interest, but doing so is not nearly as profitable.

Of course, if you want to be a proofster and demand photographic evidence of a cash-stuffed briefcase being surreptitiously handed to Suckling, sorry I guess I got nothin.

Bingo.

Lisa and I have chosen investors who are happy with just some of it, while having no control over our direction. It frees us from having to go down dark alleys and make decisions which we’d rather not have exposed in the light of day.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
I have discussed reviewers getting samples before. I must say, I am probably more restrictive than many on this board. Saying that it doesn’t matter as long as the reviewers are honest is to misunderstand the whole point of conflict of interest rules, which are about limiting situations in which such conflict might occur and not about constraining individuals. It’s also true that reviewing different kinds of things creates different needs and constraints on getting free product. The New York Times pays for admissions for all its reviewers to films and plays, which is probably best, though small papers cannot really do that. On the other hand, they can all look askance at reviewers becoming too friendly with directors and performers and they do. Book reviewers of necessity receive free copies, even for books they don’t end up reviewing, because the publishing companies desire to have unknown books reviewed far exceeds the almost null financial loss they take by setting aside a certain portion of their print run for reviewers copies. Again, when a reviewer has any kind of prior relationship with the book or the author, though, the review will get called into question.

Although, when Parker started out as a reviewer, and the WA was a newsprint called the Baltimore and Washington Wine Advocate, he did buy what he reviewed, as his career expanded, that became impossible and he did what I expect most reviewers do, which is to review by visiting and tasting or by going to a central place and getting samples delivered. I do not really consider getting samples, in any form, much of a problem, or, perhaps, rather, it is too much a necessity to do without. I think getting bottles is rather different. Even if one only uses the remains of tasting bottles for a glass of wine at dinner, that is value added. Getting 700 bottles of classified Bordeaux, including first growths, seems to me very different from visiting Bordeaux and getting tasting samples. As I said in an earlier thread, as a necessity during a pandemic that seriously impedes travels, it may be acceptable as a pis aller, but it is far less than ideal. Indeed, when travel becomes widely possible, I would consider the choice unacceptable. Magazines that accept ads from people with whom they do business is so far over the line, they can’t see the line in the rear view mirror. Again, it doesn’t matter whether the reviewers are individually or en masse honest. The business situation is a trap waiting to spring shut. Now, each of us has the obvious right to choose who we will listen to and to decide which standards to take seriously or not, including thinking that all conflict of interest rules are a crock. But, if you believe in conflict of interest rules of any sort, accepting massive amounts of free product or advertising from the entity you are reviewing are, to my mind, obvious red flags. There’s a reason the Golden Globes lack all credibility.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
In the gray area are free samples. In the early years, I believe that Parker said he bought all the wines he reviewed. I think that policy was abandoned long, long ago, though.

How are free samples a gray area?

Barrel samples have no value. And bottled samples have zero value once they’re opened. 99.9% of all the samples sent are given away or poured out at the end of the day. Perhaps a few bottles are shared over dinner. But after a long day of tasting, the last thing most reviewers want to do is drink wine.

Samples are given in exchange for reviews. It’s a trade.

So where is the gray area?

Any reviewer or publication tasting thousands or tens of thousands of wines each year could never earn enough money to pay for all the samples they taste, especially at todays prices.

There are potential gray areas but that’s not one of them.

Love ya, Jeff, but that’s close to the most obtuse thing that I have seen you write. It’s a pure quid pro quo, and by any definition of a conflict of interest, this would be it, if the reviewer is holding himself or herself out as being independent. It’s the exact opposite; it is dependent. You being invited to the greatest Chateau for barrel samples, and being sent hundreds of bottles for tasting - gratis - has huge value that most of us will never experience and could not afford to replicate. And you get to do this year after year. As a consequence of all of these freebies, critics appear to make money off their commercial venue. Nobody would subscribe if they did not review all of these free wines.

We are big boys and girls. We know the drill here. But let’s not call it something that it is not. Or act is if the relationship is not cozy. It’s up to use to weigh that in the context of what and who we value.

I read your site often and value your contributions, so I’m not bashing here, just calling it what it is.

Well said…

posts.name: Charles Fuss
posts.cooked:
Meh… most of you are hobbiests and not pros. I worked in the wine trade for several years out of school. After a while, The last thing I wanted to do was go to another tasting with hundreds of bottles. Free Sample? Yeah, that usually went to friends while I drank a coors light

It is a job. This is not CNET where you get to keep some cheesy samsung earbuds after reviewing them… Jeff and Lisa are reviewing a consumable product where the lifespan is measured in a few days. The only perk is maybe a nice table at your local restaurant , a never ending fruit fly infestation and a 200 pound trash can full of glass you have to roll out to the road.

Full disclosure, several years out of the industry my passion for wine returned… along with my dislike for beer

Getting 700 bottles of classified Bordeaux, including first growths, seems to me very different from visiting Bordeaux and getting tasting samples. As I said in an earlier thread, as a necessity during a pandemic that seriously impedes travels, it may be acceptable as a pis aller, but it is far less than ideal. Indeed, when travel becomes widely possible, I would consider the choice unacceptable.

Then again, tasting at the estate violates some fairly fundamental precepts of sensory testing.

Ultimately, I think modern wine critiquing is at best a matter of well-intentioned, talented people trying to do the impossible under less than ideal circumstances.
With predictable results.

As opposed to, say, rusty nails, forest floor, lichen, tar, slate, petrol, sandalwood, gravel (these last two used just today by a noted winemaker to describe the flavors of one of his wines) or numerous other flavor descriptors that I have not tasted and have no desire to taste.

Agreed. I actually use many of those notes myself. I have used rusty iron on some Syrah notes as well.

The descriptors I provided as examples weren’t used as aromatic descriptors, they were used as flavor/taste descriptors. Of course, I don’t find aromatic descriptors bizarre if they are describing smells/odors — and if that is how the above descriptors had been used, I probably wouldn’t smack my head about them. But taste? Have you actually put rusty iron in your mouth and tasted it? Petrol? Tar? Lichen? Dirt from a forest? Gravel? Sandalwood? If you have, bully for you. But most humans haven’t and won’t.

posts.name: Tyler Fabian
posts.cooked:
I feel that many critics and publications have gone the way of a participation ribbon. Too much focus on the 10-point upper bound of the rating scale while simultaneously providing increasingly limited qualitative information about the wine being reviewed. It’s a very poor trend.

For example, when I see a shelf-talker with a Sucking score, I have to mentally do ‘Suckling math’ in my head to figure out where that score might be relative to other critics. For some, I take the Suckling score and subtract 10 points. Other times its 5. Or maybe as many as 15. If my palate aligned with LPB and I didn’t have to do that mental math to help me find other wines to explore, I’d subscribe in a heartbeat. Sadly, Suckling math hasn’t been nearly as complicated the last few years, as the distribution is no longer 20-30 points, but maybe 10 at best.

Also, I am not someone who cares about a high score. I care more about whether my palate aligns with the critic and what their particular score can tell me about whether the wine might be in my wheelhouse. There is too much wine out there and not nearly enough time in a day (or dollars in my pocketbook) to even pretend I have the bandwidth to navigate on my own.

Cheers to LPB and Johan for taking the risk. I’ll be watching and rooting.

I had spoken with Lisa in the past about this for the Wine Advocate which has still not come to fruition (someday, maybe someday), but I never knew if she was opposed to the model or other larger forces in the organization.

Anyway, I will plant the seed and happy to talk. It’s a lot of work on our side, but it might be good as you build out your database for you to know what potential collaboration would entail technically.

Thanks Eric,

We’ll try and snowball best we can and see what the future can bring. Yeah, there are a lot of things people have misunderstood re Lisa. Not as easy as some imagine to have suits prodding you while trying to keep the audience happy. She’s been living between a rock and a hard place, and dealing with it, for more years than most would endure.

If life were only as simple as everything being black and white (says the devoutly monochrome photographer)

Cheers,

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
I have no idea on rusty nails, but surely you know what some of these other things smell like, right? Things we “taste” in wine are largely aromatic compounds, after all. Most of the terms you mentioned seem like totally reasonable descriptors to me, even if petrol is sort of shorthand (I say TDN when I think the people I’m talking to will understand that).

Agreed. I actually use many of those notes myself. I have used rusty iron on some Syrah notes as well.

The descriptors I provided as examples weren’t used as aromatic descriptors, they were used as flavor/taste descriptors. Of course, I don’t find aromatic descriptors bizarre if they are describing smells/odors — and if that is how the above descriptors had been used, I probably wouldn’t smack my head about them. But taste? Have you actually put rusty iron in your mouth and tasted it? Petrol? Tar? Lichen? Dirt from a forest? Gravel? Sandalwood? If you have, bully for you. But most humans haven’t and won’t.

Open mouth, insert foot.

Look up “retronasal olfaction”.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
I have no idea on rusty nails, but surely you know what some of these other things smell like, right? Things we “taste” in wine are largely aromatic compounds, after all. Most of the terms you mentioned seem like totally reasonable descriptors to me, even if petrol is sort of shorthand (I say TDN when I think the people I’m talking to will understand that).

Agreed. I actually use many of those notes myself. I have used rusty iron on some Syrah notes as well.

The descriptors I provided as examples weren’t used as aromatic descriptors, they were used as flavor/taste descriptors. Of course, I don’t find aromatic descriptors bizarre if they are describing smells/odors — and if that is how the above descriptors had been used, I probably wouldn’t smack my head about them. But taste? Have you actually put rusty iron in your mouth and tasted it? Petrol? Tar? Lichen? Dirt from a forest? Gravel? Sandalwood? If you have, bully for you. But most humans haven’t and won’t.

To expand on Wes’s response a bit, most of what we think we are tasting in wine (and in many foods and beverages), we are actually smelling. Our brains interpret a lot of aromas as taste, falsely, when the signals come from things in our mouths. But yes, look up retronasal olfaction to learn more. The point is that it’s totally appropriate to use aroma descriptors to talk about the way something seems to taste. The next time your nose is so stuffed up that you can’t breathe through it at all, try a sip of a wine you’re familiar with and see how that compares to what you’re used to. I do think some descriptors that are used are not helpful to almost anyone other than the person writing the note (see critic bingo thread), but I don’t agree that the examples you gave fall into that category.

posts.name: CF Binnig
posts.cooked:
Agreed. I actually use many of those notes myself. I have used rusty iron on some Syrah notes as well.

Of course, I don’t find aromatic descriptors bizarre if they are describing smells/odors — and if that is how the above descriptors had been used, I probably wouldn’t smack my head about them. But taste? Have you actually put rusty iron in your mouth and tasted it? Petrol? Tar? Lichen? Dirt from a forest? Gravel? Sandalwood? If you have, bully for you. But most humans haven’t and won’t.

To expand on Wes’s response a bit, most of what we think we are tasting in wine (and in many foods and beverages), we are actually smelling. Our brains interpret a lot of aromas as taste, falsely, when the signals come from things in our mouths. But yes, look up retronasal olfaction to learn more. The point is that it’s totally appropriate to use aroma descriptors to talk about the way something seems to taste. The next time your nose is so stuffed up that you can’t breathe through it at all, try a sip of a wine you’re familiar with and see how that compares to what you’re used to. I do think some descriptors that are used are not helpful to almost anyone other than the person writing the note (see critic bingo thread), but I don’t agree that the examples you gave fall into that category.

I’ve been drinking wine for 40 years and collecting it for 30. I’ve read most of the literature on the taste/smell connection. I still think that aromatic descriptors should be separated from taste descriptors, and the taste descriptors used should reflect things we normally put in our mouths and eat/drink/taste. Using aromatic descriptors as a substitute for taste descriptors is, in my mind, arguably lazy/sloppy/undisciplined or an attempt to be obscure and elitist, but putting that aside, it is unhelpful to most consumers and is one of the reasons many consumers make fun of wine reviewers.

posts.name: Robert Grenley
posts.cooked:
Since we are talking about descriptors, I personally find aromatic and taste descriptors in wine reviews to be a complete waste of time. I would be more interested in such things as depth of color, intensity of aromas, complexity and depth of flavors, acidity and freshness, degree of ripeness, indicators of over ripeness and excessive alcohol, length of finish, overall balance and level of quality for its level, etc.

I may be alone in this, but I don’t think I have ever purchased (or passed on) a wine based on what endlessly boring set of specific plant, animal, or mineral aromas and flavors the wine writer perceived in the wine. Perhaps I am mistaken, but this stuffing of tasting notes with endless descriptors seemed to really take off with Parker, and accelerate thereafter. To me it smacks of trying to show off one’s tasting acumen and experience.

Agreed, though Jilly Goolden in the UK (and to a lesser degree Oz Clarke) were often ridiculed for their fruit salad wine descriptions on the Food & Drink TV show that started in 1982 so at least a similar era to Parker.

The more structural elements were something Ric Einstein (aka TORB, or as he was known on TWA: *** ******** ) made a conscious effort with in his tasting sheet / notes, and someone like Michael Schuster really brings out in his essential winetasting book.

Much of that is still personal judgement / experience, but nonetheless I feel what you’ve listed are good elements to consider - not in every tasting note, but yes where they are notable. The exception in that for me is acidity, as I’ll almost always mention it, even if it’s simply supportive (e.g. not lacking nor biting).

This is such a weird take. Why would I not comment on a flavor (or retronasal “aroma”) as part of the palate/drinking process when that’s when it presented itself? You say that “this is why we make fun of wine reviewers” but attempting to separate out retronasal olfaction in a tasting note is both impossible from a sensory perspective and also way more snobby and ridiculous.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
I’ve always tended to taste wine through the process of looking at the wine (eye), sniffing it (nose) and eventually tasting it (palate). Inevitably that structure usually follows through in the tasting note.

I’m not sure from the above whether that makes me lazy or snobby. I would hope it were neither.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
Since we are talking about descriptors, I personally find aromatic and taste descriptors in wine reviews to be a complete waste of time. I would be more interested in such things as depth of color, intensity of aromas, complexity and depth of flavors, acidity and freshness, degree of ripeness, indicators of over ripeness and excessive alcohol, length of finish, overall balance and level of quality for its level, etc.

I may be alone in this, but I don’t think I have ever purchased (or passed on) a wine based on what endlessly boring set of specific plant, animal, or mineral aromas and flavors the wine writer perceived in the wine. Perhaps I am mistaken, but this stuffing of tasting notes with endless descriptors seemed to really take off with Parker, and accelerate thereafter. To me it smacks of trying to show off one’s tasting acumen and experience.

You may not be alone in that, but there are others who are interested in those descriptors, as there are certain cue’s that they might be looking for. For example, if one is getting priced out of Cote Rotie or Cornas, looking for other French syrah driven wines that highlight smoky/meaty/peppery notes might be of interest. Or if one wants a (cheaper!) Condrieu like clone to open midweek, looking for notes in viognier blends that highlight peach and floral in higher viscosity, lower acid frames might be of interest.

Of course everyone perceives flavors differently, so the elusive ‘singed alderwood’ might just be ‘smoky’ to others, if they even notice it. If a professional critic is offering reviews with the old British style of ‘Very Fine Indeed’, I’m not sure how to use that, and thus what the commercial value of that might be to merchant licensing or individual subscribers.

posts.name: Cris Whetstone
posts.cooked:
A bunch of fruit descriptors are certainly not necessary. They can indicate ripeness levels for certain grapes.

The more secondary characters can be useful in notes as they might indicate the style of a wine. Things like graphite, pepper, mineral, etc are not likely to show up in more modern, fruit forward wines. Some of these can also be indications of oak treatments like cinnamon or vanilla.

While I don’t really want a lengthy set of descriptors in my notes they can be useful. Given a trusted taster of course.

I view them as suggested approaches, not rules. But yes, saying something “tastes like shit” has always struck me as odd, since I have never tasted shit, and I would like to think that the user of the phrase hasn’t tasted shit either. But “smells like shit”, I think I can get behind that.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
I view them as suggested approaches, not rules. But yes, saying something “tastes like shit” has always struck me as odd, since I have never tasted shit, and I would like to think that the user of the phrase hasn’t tasted shit either. But “smells like shit”, I think I can get behind that.

But, if you’re smelling something that’s in your mouth (via the back of your throat) and it smells like something you wouldn’t put in your mouth…so what? It smells like what it smells like. So, if you’re drinking a wine and there’s a distinct retronasal aroma that recalls something specific to you, but that’s something you wouldn’t put in your mouth (whether pleasant or not of a smell), you would endeavor to describe it less accurately than you could because of this weird hangup you have???

E: Not to mention the idea that we can only use “normal” taste descriptors to describe taste is really exclusionary. I think way more people would understand a “rusted iron” descriptor than a “balut” descriptor. Which one of those do you normally put in YOUR mouth?

Impossible to separate, you say? Hmmmmm. I guess these scientists must be wasting their time: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154320300259. And while I’ve never put rusted iron or a balut in my mouth, if I were to survey the entire spectrum of humanity, I’d bet the latter is more familiar than the former, as it is something certain people actually eat.

You’re using a study showing the interaction between retronasal olfaction and taste to argue against them being “impossible to separate”?

Of course that’s data being sent from you various receptors to the sensory processing area of your brain. Add in tactile sensations to the mix. Each additional piece of data can potentially affect the perception of all other data.

Also note the array of about 80 types of smell receptors an aroma hits from your throat/back of your mouth is going to be different than those an aroma hits via your nose. And, also note some aroma compounds are created or released in reaction with saliva. Because of that, some wine smells you can only pick up retronasally. So, all these factors add up to some wines being perceived quite differently on the nose than in the mouth. Some.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Looks like LPB is in business. Sort of. She is now on the Pix wine platform plugging various California wines. Napa Valley Collectibles for Under $100- Lisa Perrotti-Brown

I’m not sure whether I buy the idea that a Signorello chardonnay is “collectible,” though.

Pix, which was launched last year, bills itself as:

… the world’s first wine discovery platform that makes buying the right wine for the right occasion a delight — and champions the wine industry by making it more equitable for vintners and retailers of all sizes to sell. With a clever wine search engine alongside inviting articles written by diverse voices in the wine world, Pix provides curious wine drinkers with a joyful and pretense-free way to explore wine, no matter their expertise or their budget. The company was founded in Napa, CA by an industry-vetted team spanning wine and tech, led by digital wine innovator Paul Mabray.

Hmm. Sounds a lot like Wine Searcher.

It says they don’t take a cut on sales made via clicks from their site and they don’t charge to list wines there. (They’ll upload a store’s catalog on request.) Not sure what the business model is, then, unless the idea is to phase in charges to the trade over time.

The most important word for Lisa and me with TWI is transparency. That The Wine Independent can be taken at face value.

It is up to the consumer to decide where the line between independence and non-independence goes. How much is too much? And are you being told how the business is ran?

What we are offering is simply transparency so that the consumer can make that choice. We’re not going to become hermits. We will not stop living life. We are quite open with that and where we draw the line. Up to you to decide if we’ve drawn it at the right place and compare it to others, as much as they’ll let you. There is no question you can put to us about what we do which we will not answer.

Robert Joseph has some thoughts if you’d like:

meiningers-international.com

FUTUREWATCH: Critic Publishers | Meininger's International

As the number of subscription-funded online wine reviewers grows, questions are being asked about the generosity and integrity of some of their scores. Robert Joseph reports  

All the best,

posts.name: Peter Chiu

What a very unusual subset of the wine community. I wish you good fortune in your venture.

Blessed be the fermented fruit.

At TWI (The Winemaid Independent) all shall wear red frocks and white victorian bonnets.

Your idea…of doing a vertical of La Grande Rue…is not just fun but brilliant

Well, I can assure you that the registration of interest on the site has been well beyond our (and presumably your) expectations.

Another encouraging sign is how the news of TWI has been received in Bordeaux. People are genuinely happy to finally be able to talk about the business side of wine criticism in an honest way. And once you get them started it’s hard for them to hold back. Their frustration was always there. They are genuinely happy that Lisa has taken this step away from it all. Hi Siun,

Thanks for the kind words.

Just to be clear, and as stated earlier also in this thread, we will not be paying for the vast majority of samples. That is just DC Universe kind of thinking. If you ever read a review of 1947 Lafleur, 1900 Margaux or 2001 Harlan in TWI you can rest assured that our reviewer most likely will not have paid for any of those bottles. Neither will any other leading reviewer at any other publication. If ones idea of a perfect world is one where you can read reviews of rare and expensive wines, or for that part hundreds of en primeur samples, where the wines were not offered as freebees, then you are out of luck because you will not find it.

We will also as stated have private and commercial (reprint, multiple licenses) subscription options.

We love events and want to engage the public. They will be transparent in how they function and producers will never pay to attend or have a table.

Face value all round.

All the best,
