I’m not proposing the tasting because I have a lot of lamarche and want the wines to go up in value. Sure I have a couple cases of LGR but I have a lot of almost all burgundy producers.

I’m proposing the tasting because I think it would be interesting and would potentially change some people’s preconceived notions about LGR. If you aren’t interested in a blind tasting of La Tache and La Grande Rue, I’m sure there will be plenty of people that will be.

With all due respect to Dr. Kelley, I don’t really care what his (or Greg’s, yours, or anyone else’s) opinions are about wines I’ve had. If I like them, I’m going to buy them.

I find critics useful at times to evaluate new producers I haven’t had the opportunity to try especially if they are hard to source and sometimes they are excellent sources of information about vineyards, ownership, or industry gossip, which is why I subscribe to jasper Morris, nanson’s site, and TWA but that’s really the extent of it and appreciate feature articles where they speak with winemakers but couldn’t care less about scores.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
FWIW I think it’s about time we stopped lauding the ‘machismo’ of tasting 100+ wines every day that became an emblem of pride for TWA. Less is more.

Fair enough of a point. But…

I have been exposed to more critics than most. Thankfully there are no quarantine restrictions for being in close contact! Not an infectious enough mutation around yet.

From that experience I believe that not all critics are made from the same mold. Some can taste 100+ wines in a day and produce notes which are, shall we say, sparse… Others do 40+ and deliver notes of literary canon. Then there are the ones who can do both. They do not slow down or tire mentally. For them it is not machismo as it does not faze them to do something which mere mortals think is a gargantuan task. It’s simply all in a days work. It is a rare talent.

My own private little notes are all done on my phone and have a limit of 15 words before you ask. I can do 100+ wines on a good day, but the notes are eventually down to just a single word at that stage usually. I’m better off snapping 500 photographs a day.

Less can sometimes be more, then again, so can more.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Hi Johan
Thanks for the response, however I’m going to be doubly difficult in railing against this concept that critics have a ‘rare talent’, or that ‘mere mortals’ would think it a gargantuan task, but it’s merely ‘all in a days work’ for this rare talent.

Parker made similar claims to having such a perfect palate, using it to encourage people to blindly believe him, because of his claims. It worked for quite a while, helping him to commercial success. In latter years people saw through such claims.

If we’re talking about raising the ethical standards of the profession, then I would strongly recommend dropping such claims to be a rare talent. The truly ethical critic doesn’t claim superpowers, nor use it to make the reader feel inadequate. A tasting note / review should not be an instruction to buy (or not) from the ‘guru’, but more an educated/informed insight into helping the reader make their own mind up. To write in that style is much more ethical and I would encourage that rather than in any way trying to put the critic on a pedestal.

Regards
Ian

posts.name: RH Drexel
posts.cooked:
Johan - understood. The key is always transparency and it sounds like you are making that commitment which is what matters.

It is a tenet we will have to live by (although hopefully in a non Christopher Nolan way)

Also, I think transparency is something the consumer absolutely has a right to expect of the people to whom they give their money and trust. It can not be compromised. Or claimed it can not be given because of some opaque greater good. I mean, just look at the Byrds and how that is working out in the Ozarks…

It has so far been a very interesting exercise to take part in this thread. Much has been made of Lisa on a personal level rather than comparing what TWI is in reality proposing in comparison to what others are. I don’t know if it is a trait in common with the American election system where people tend to vote on what they think of personality rather than party and policy (a Swedish trait).

I think all questions have been fair however, and they are welcome.

And, if you’d like to know my political leaning, it is Champagne Socialist.

I’ve been around for 6 decades or so. Heard marketing bullshit like yours for at least 3 generations or so. Glad I can ignore it. But keep at it, because it will probably pay the rent for you for at least a decade or so until the current marks catch on. Cheers!

Michael

“Glad I can ignore it.”

Subsequently doesn’t ignore it.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
FWIW I think it’s about time we stopped lauding the ‘machismo’ of tasting 100+ wines every day that became an emblem of pride for TWA. Less is more.

Fair enough of a point. But…

I have been exposed to more critics than most. Thankfully there are no quarantine restrictions for being in close contact! Not an infectious enough mutation around yet.

From that experience I believe that not all critics are made from the same mold. Some can taste 100+ wines in a day and produce notes which are, shall we say, sparse… Others do 40+ and deliver notes of literary canon. Then there are the ones who can do both. They do not slow down or tire mentally. For them it is not machismo as it does not faze them to do something which mere mortals think is a gargantuan task. It’s simply all in a days work. It is a rare talent.

My own private little notes are all done on my phone and have a limit of 15 words before you ask. I can do 100+ wines on a good day, but the notes are eventually down to just a single word at that stage usually. I’m better off snapping 500 photographs a day.

Less can sometimes be more, then again, so can more.

I disagree with you completely. Critics work their behinds off and every one of them that I have met has been intelligent and dedicated. Watching David Schildknecht taste was a lot of fun, and he has an exceptional palate. Josh Raynolds is a taster of REMARKABLE fortitude. Others as well. But…

It may be a rare talent to write voluminous notes on 100+ wines tasted in a single day. That doesn’t mean that their palates are truly capable of absorbing the beating that tasting that much wine is, and then retain the same discerning abilities that they would if tasting a more modest number.

Super tasters are real. So is palate fatigue. And so is the reality that alcohol is a depressant, and if you bathe your taste buds in it 100+ times a day, you will lose your ability to discern the finer nuances in wines. Tannin build is also real. It happens within 6 wines. I have seen this in a group of 50 professional tasters. People who publicly overstate their abilities to do a task are also real…just look at politics.

Unicorns are not real.

And these days, I make my tasting appointments with critics early or not at all.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
With all due respect to Dr. Kelley, I don’t really care what his (or Greg’s, yours, or anyone else’s) opinions are about wines I’ve had. If I like them, I’m going to buy them.

You absolutely should.

Equally, walking through the vineyards, there are differences in the quality of viticulture and vine genetics between Lamarche and their better neighbors that help me make sense of the differences that I find in the glass—differences that, for me, the zip code can’t efface.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
With all due respect to Dr. Kelley, I don’t really care what his (or Greg’s, yours, or anyone else’s) opinions are about wines I’ve had. If I like them, I’m going to buy them.

I wouldn’t presume to tell anyone what wines to buy or not to buy. The only rule of wine is to drink wines that people like. If you like Lamarche, you should buy Lamarche. That’s where that discussion ends.

However, you’re making the argument that the general quality of Lamarche and DRC are similar and that people would not be able to tell them apart at a blind tasting. You’ve also suggested that people don’t like Lamarche because of preconceptions. None of those things are subjective opinions and I don’t think given the difference in winemaking (among other things) they’re true. (Most of the Lamarche I’ve has been at blind tastings - I drink a lot of wine blind because I think it’s both fun and a good way to avoid preconceptions.)

If you want to do a vertical of 15-19 La Tache vs. Grand Rue, they’re your wines, by all means! They’re your wines. Personally, I think it would be a waste and I wouldn’t find it a very instructive exercise.

Hi Johan
Thanks for the response, however I’m going to be doubly difficult in railing against this concept that critics have a ‘rare talent’, or that ‘mere mortals’ would think it a gargantuan task, but it’s merely ‘all in a days work’ for this rare talent.

Ian

No problem Ian,

I think that we actually agree on certain points while coming at them from different perspectives,

I am in no way suggesting “a perfect palete” as you say or that anyone should follow blindly just because a critic can taste a certain amount of wines. It is more complex than that.

It has nothing to do with making a reader out to be inadequate. In fact it is the opposite. A truly great critic is humble in the understanding that all they offer is advice and not absolute knowledge. They are not gurus in their own eyes. They understand that a reader may come to completely different conclusions and they respect that.

I think that is one of the aims we have with TWI. We want to give advice and let our readers find wines which suit them irrespective of score. That is what our filters and tasting notes will hopefully provide.

Where we might disagree is on the rare talent part. In all fields there is such a thing. Wine tasting is not an exception. All pursuits have a small group who the majority will never be able to match. As I have said, that does not mean that there are not tasters who can be of the same caliber at a slower pace. But to ignore that some can actually do what they just simply do, is to ignore fact.

So, in the end, I am quadrupley sure that we are probably on the same page to a large degree.

Cheers,

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng

With all due respect to Dr. Kelley, I don’t really care what his (or Greg’s, yours, or anyone else’s) opinions are about wines I’ve had. If I like them, I’m going to buy them.

You absolutely should.

Equally, walking through the vineyards, there are differences in the quality of viticulture and vine genetics between Lamarche and their better neighbors that help me make sense of the differences that I find in the glass—differences that, for me, the zip code can’t efface.

While I certainly appreciate that viticulture is an important part of the wine production process and has an effect on the quality, as well as barrel selection, elevage, wine making, etc; it raises some questions.

In the past, from what I’ve read, a lot of these considerations were thought of less, and things like organic/biodynamic winemaking didn’t exist. Chapitalization was much more common.

Yet, lots of phenomenal wines were made from the 50-80s.

Is the thought that wine made today will be incrementally better than the wines from the past in 20-30 years?

I have my doubts. We’ve already seen issues arising from supposed process improvements in white burgundy. This isn’t even considering the future challenges from climate change.

posts.name: Tom G l a s g o w
posts.cooked:
Fair enough of a point. But…

I have been exposed to more critics than most. Thankfully there are no quarantine restrictions for being in close contact! Not an infectious enough mutation around yet.

From that experience I believe that not all critics are made from the same mold. Some can taste 100+ wines in a day and produce notes which are, shall we say, sparse… Others do 40+ and deliver notes of literary canon. Then there are the ones who can do both. They do not slow down or tire mentally. For them it is not machismo as it does not faze them to do something which mere mortals think is a gargantuan task. It’s simply all in a days work. It is a rare talent.

My own private little notes are all done on my phone and have a limit of 15 words before you ask. I can do 100+ wines on a good day, but the notes are eventually down to just a single word at that stage usually. I’m better off snapping 500 photographs a day.

Less can sometimes be more, then again, so can more.

I disagree with you completely. Critics work their behinds off and every one of them that I have met has been intelligent and dedicated. Watching David Schildknecht taste was a lot of fun, and he has an exceptional palate. Josh Raynolds is a taster of REMARKABLE fortitude. Others as well. But…

It may be a rare talent to write voluminous notes on 100+ wines tasted in a single day. That doesn’t mean that their palates are truly capable of absorbing the beating that tasting that much wine is, and then retain the same discerning abilities that they would if tasting a more modest number.

Super tasters are real. So is palate fatigue. And so is the reality that alcohol is a depressant, and if you bathe your taste buds in it 100+ times a day, you will lose your ability to discern the finer nuances in wines. Tannin build is also real. It happens within 6 wines. I have seen this in a group of 50 professional tasters. People who publicly overstate their abilities to do a task are also real…just look at politics.

Unicorns are not real.

And these days, I make my tasting appointments with critics early or not at all.

Hmm, not quite sure why you think you and I disagree on the fact that critics “work their behinds off” or that they are “intelligent and dedicated”. But it was certainly not from anything I’ve written. So, we are actually on agreement for this part. Great.

For your second point, well, then we do in the end disagree. Your stance strikes me as too much leaning towards Dogma or Die. You have a set idea, and your parameters will not allow for outliers.

I believe that there is no reason to think that wine tasting would be different from any other field in the world. There will always be exceptions to the “rules”. They will be few, but they will exist. To think that wine tasting would be the only space where this does not happen would make it the most unicorn of all.

I think winemakers should be all in on dogma in their winemaking. People talk about learning a lot by following a wine through its maturity. Who follows a wine through its maturity better than a winemaker? This is why I like Kelley dabbling in winemaking.
posts.display_username: Tom G l a s g o w

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked: I recently did an exploration of Lamarche across the range. It was all ‘18 since that’s what was available in my neighborhood. For me, these might be the best example of a rising tide lifting all boats. Maybe they are a little better than 20 years ago, and maybe the prices are slightly less inflated on a relative basis to other burgundy producers, but the wines are still mediocre. If you care about relative price, I could see an argument for paying for the malconsorts or vosne village - they compete price wise with similarly priced mediocre wines that are at the same place in the hierarchy. The grand rue is highway robbery at $600. Its a ok at best and I don’t see a drastic change in outlook for that domaine than where it was 20 years ago. You could buy top tier burgundy for that price.

posts.name: Cris Whetstone
posts.cooked:
At the risk of interrupting tedious Burgundy disagreement #256,492, I think the tasting lots of wines per day and being able to have some ability to discern them is mostly about practice and focus. It only becomes a so-called talent by doing the work.

There is certainly going to be some level of different between individuals in terms of sensitivity to aromas and flavors, not to mention textures and sensations. I don’t believe those differences are as big as to make some people rare talents in their fields. For the most part its the repetition like it is for most skills in life. You gotta do the work.

If you set out to taste a lot and be focused enough to try lots of wine your skill in that area is going to go up. If you want to learn it and have access to do so you will gain those skills.

There has also got to be some upper limit on an individual as to what they can do effectively with those skills. Fatigue and exhaustion are a fact of life in most any endeavor. Sometimes we just have to admit it to ourselves.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Tasting is easy. Discernibly translating the nuance into a unique, accurate, descriptive and usable short paragraph is the rub

At 10-25 wines, tasting is easy.

At 100+, it’s not. Super tasters taste things more intensely, but alcohol is alcohol.

And context is huge. Why did the rise of high extraction, high alcohol, over oaked, bombastic wines occur with the advent of the massive tastings?

posts.name: RH Drexel
posts.cooked:
Tasting is easy. Discernibly translating the nuance into a unique, accurate, descriptive and usable short paragraph is the rub

At 10-25 wines, tasting is easy.

At 100+, it’s not. Super tasters taste things more intensely, but alcohol is alcohol.

And context is huge. Why did the rise of high extraction, high alcohol, over oaked, bombastic wines occur with the advent of the massive tastings?

In the 70’s, stateside at least, we had disco and classic rock and roll. The 80’s gave us punk and new wave. The 90’s, grunge and hip hop, and so on.

Critics do not create cultural shifts, movements and trends, they merely report on them. The pendulum swings periodically between styles and extremes in nearly every sector of human interest: food, wine, music, art, fashion, etc.

To suggest that big, extracted wines exist because of massive tastings is to ignore the every-changing tsstes of the consumer, shifts in the way that vineyard management companies farm their vineyards, stylistic choices by individual winemakers, and a myriad of other influences.

You’re giving the critic and these tastings way too much power, when, all along, most of the power has resided with the consumer.

You’re giving the critic and these tastings way too much power, when, all along, most of the power has resided with the consumer.

Well, as Ian wrote (or strongly implied) RMP was therefore a guru rather than a critic. Wittingly or not he certainly inspired winemakers to change the style of their wines. Whether he followed fashion or fashion followed him is another chicken vs the egg story!

As for the massive tastings, it seems logical to conclude that the style change was no coincidence - they wanted his attention and the only way to stand out was to push the volume up to eleven! It was hardly surprising - a couple of points at the time could make the difference, for example, between a successful EP campaign and a successful Foires aux Vins campaign, with lots of digits at stake.

Nowadays there are far more critics than before and no real pecking order, so I agree things have become like they are in the other sectors. So much the better.

As for LPB, it’s going to be interesting to see if her taste has changed - at TWA she was very much in the Parker template, but I don’t know whether it was to protect the brand or not.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked:
In the 70’s, stateside at least, we had disco and classic rock and roll. The 80’s gave us punk and new wave. The 90’s, grunge and hip hop, and so on.

Critics do not create cultural shifts, movements and trends, they merely report on them. The pendulum swings periodically between styles and extremes in nearly every sector of human interest: food, wine, music, art, fashion, etc.

To suggest that big, extracted wines exist because of massive tastings is to ignore the every-changing tsstes of the consumer, shifts in the way that vineyard management companies farm their vineyards, stylistic choices by individual winemakers, and a myriad of other influences.

You’re giving the critic and these tastings way too much power, when, all along, most of the power has resided with the consumer.

There are numerous examples of critics creating tastes and not merely reporting on them. Parker’s success doing this is what led the Atlantic Monthly to call him the most powerful critic in the world. Clement Greenberg is an example from the art world. That’s not to say that any of the current crop of critics has the same power or is committed enough to something to exercise that power even if they did have it. But to talk about consumer tastes as totally independent from what the critics are pushing, when tens of millions of bottles are still moved blindly based on point ratings, doesn’t ring very true.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
In the 70’s, stateside at least, we had disco and classic rock and roll. The 80’s gave us punk and new wave. The 90’s, grunge and hip hop, and so on.

Critics do not create cultural shifts, movements and trends, they merely report on them. The pendulum swings periodically between styles and extremes in nearly every sector of human interest: food, wine, music, art, fashion, etc.

To suggest that big, extracted wines exist because of massive tastings is to ignore the every-changing tsstes of the consumer, shifts in the way that vineyard management companies farm their vineyards, stylistic choices by individual winemakers, and a myriad of other influences.

You’re giving the critic and these tastings way too much power, when, all along, most of the power has resided with the consumer.

I think that’s just wrong as a factual matter vis-a-vis wine.

Parker’s influence became huge as the 80s progressed. Over the course of a decade, his scores to a very significant degree determined what sold and at what price at the upper end of the market. Anyone in the trade in that time will confirm that.

I read the WA avidly over those years and tasted blindly many times a month (sometimes several times a week), and I observed how his palate shifted toward bigger and bigger wines. He had never shown much appreciation for leaner wines (e.g., Burgundy), but there was a shift. His top scores went to very big wines, whether in Bordeaux, California or the Rhones – the areas where he was most passionate.

It was in that period that I heard from friends in the trade that he was regularly tasting 50 and 70 wines at a tasting. At one point, he published an article on California chardonnays with just scores – no descriptions!

The wine makers followed his lead.

Zind-Humbrecht changed the style of its wines after Parker went gaga over a vintage (1989, as I recall) when all the ZH wines showed residual sugar.

At a vertical tasting in 1999 with a renowned traditionalist French producer where Parker spoke, I told the winemaker how much I appreciated his wines. With Parker maybe 10 feet away, the winemaker leaned in and said, “It’s getting harder and harder to make wines like this because of certain critics.”

I follow Chateauneuf less closely, but I think his enthusiasm for the most bulbous of those also shaped winemaking there and helped drive up prices dramatically.

Meanwhile, (Big) Jay Miller, working for Parker, was giving 95+ to 15+% Australian wines. That certainly drove the market for Aussie wines in the US for a period in the mid-2000s.

Of course, some people will naturally enjoy fruity, forward wines that may even seem a bit sweet (because of high ABV, even without RS). But to claim that consumer tastes drove the shift toward very big wines ignores the crucial role of Parker points in selling wine. I agree that in music movies and some other areas, critics have much less influence. But in wine, Parker and the scores he handed out to the ripest wines directly altered many wineries’ styles.

This is literally exactly what happened with Parker and even if you didn’t see it firsthand (like me) you can see the effects, and hear the numerous stories in all corners of the the wine industry.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
In the 70’s, stateside at least, we had disco and classic rock and roll. The 80’s gave us punk and new wave. The 90’s, grunge and hip hop, and so on.

Critics do not create cultural shifts, movements and trends, they merely report on them. The pendulum swings periodically between styles and extremes in nearly every sector of human interest: food, wine, music, art, fashion, etc.

To suggest that big, extracted wines exist because of massive tastings is to ignore the every-changing tsstes of the consumer, shifts in the way that vineyard management companies farm their vineyards, stylistic choices by individual winemakers, and a myriad of other influences.

You’re giving the critic and these tastings way too much power, when, all along, most of the power has resided with the consumer.

There are numerous examples of critics creating tastes and not merely reporting on them. Parker’s success doing this is what led the Atlantic Monthly to call him the most powerful critic in the world. Clement Greenberg is an example from the art world. That’s not to say that any of the current crop of critics has the same power or is committed enough to something to exercise that power even if they did have it. But to talk about consumer tastes as totally independent from what the critics are pushing, when tens of millions of bottles are still moved blindly based on point ratings, doesn’t ring very true.

It happens, but usually only when the market is confined enough and, for one reason or another, one critic gets an outsized megaphone. There are examples of influential book and movie critics but none that move the market. The market is large enough for word of mouth to overcome any individual taste. Even voices as distinct as Pauline Kael are mostly influential within circles too small to drive the success of movies.The theater critic for the NY Times, however, has an outsized voice because the audience is relatively small and the number of voices to compete with that critic are too few. In Greenberg’s case, he was virtually the only one who could make sense of abstraction in the 40s and 50s–well, Rosenberg was a meaningful alternative, and he, too, had influence at the time, but Greenberg’s explanation has worn better for numbers of reasons–and so his voice carried a lot of weight. In Parker’s case, he was the first, and for a long time the only, critic who wrote for anyone who was interested as opposed to the rather inbred English aristocracy of criticism at the time. No one will ever wield his influence again, I expect, which is all to the good.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
There has also got to be some upper limit on an individual as to what they can do effectively with those skills. Fatigue and exhaustion are a fact of life in most any endeavor. Sometimes we just have to admit it to ourselves.

Actually they don’t.

There’s no factual check of how degraded a persons palate is. Just their own opinion of how well they’re doing.

FWIW the late (Daniel) Rogov did introduce a process to monitor for this when tasting in his ‘office’. From memory, he instructed his staff to plant duplicate wines in the line-up (same wine/bottle with a 2nd glass of it presented later in the same day, as with all the others, blind).

I think his criteria was that if the 2nd glass was scored + or - 2 points from the 1st, his assistant would inform him, and he’d stop tasting for the day.

It’s still a rather rudimentary check, and like most critics, his range of scores wasn’t especially wide, but nonetheless at least he made the effort to try and bring some sort of control into proceedings.

Sauternes is my favourite wine in a global sense, and from my personal vantage point Climens and Rieussec are the ones closest in the running.

That said, and to prove that I do not have constant blinders on but rather do try and keep an open mind… A wine which absolutely blew my camera bags off, and which I was 100% sure on being Yquem, was actually not even a Sauternes at all. More than happy in fact.

Hi Jonathan,

As I’ve stated a few times by now, I am most doubtful that Berserkers is a place where we will find much subscriber base of importance, and advertising here is most likely not a revenue generating operation.

Having said that, there was indeed some interest expressed by participants in what we would look like both in text and design and also direct requests for photographs being shown. Storytelling is a true passion of mine and there will be a free to view section of TWI in the future. More than anything it is the stories I have been pointing to rather than the scores which you are more than free to ignore. My hope is that you will find something of value from what the winemakers have to say irrespective of all other things and just enjoy the added knowledge for what it is, simply sharing.

And I’ve been enjoying the back and forth, which has been much more civilised than the Berserker reputation IRL would indicate. It’s a fun place.

You can rest assured that I will never attempt to sell a subscription point blank in here once we actually have any to sell.

All the best,

If you are a critic (of anything) and you move the needle - you get paid. End of story. You don’t need someone else’s job.

Your venture is going to be a case study in this department. Does your critic, who was presumably moving the the needle while at TWA, carry the same power without the TWA name behind the review.

My guess is no, and really for no other reason other than the most powerful reviews now are crowdsourced. There are so many individual datapoints on each wine that people now simply don’t turn to one “trusted source”. People also sprinkle in a little of their own confirmation bias, and seek out the review that best suits their purpose.

Want to know if 2019 Stewart Cellars Dr. Crane is worth purchasing at $175? Lisa wrote:

“Deep garnet-purple colored, the 2019 Nomad Cabernet Sauvignon Beckstoffer Dr. Crane explodes with blackcurrant cordial, fruitcake and blueberry preserves scents, plus hints of cast-iron pan, black truffles and tree bark. Full-bodied, concentrated and oh-so velvety, it has an electric line of freshness supporting the many layers of rich, hedonic fruit, finishing very long and earthy. - 98 points”

Go over to Vinous and Antonio wrote:

“The 2019 Cabernet Sauvignon Nomad Beckstoffer’s Dr. Crane Vineyard is overripe and pruney, but also not as harsh as some of the other wines in this range. Inky dark fruit, chocolate and espresso are all kicked up here. 80 points”

It’s like they didn’t even taste the same wine. So people are going to take those two reviews, and go find 2, 3 or even more write-ups online. Stuff from pros, stuff from amateurs, and then make an assessment based on all they have seen.

That is what makes it harder and harder to become the go to trusted source for all things wine like Parker once was…

Johnny consumer doesn’t care if the winery hosted a lunch for critic A who gave it 93 points and that critic B bought it at the local shop and gave it 89 points and critic C had a free sample mailed to them and tasted it in their living room out and gave it 98.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
Those Fortune pieces and the whole “Wine Independent” rap have me rolling my eyes. There’s already a place to go for truly independent tasting notes - it’s called Cellartracker. People who work as professional wine critics can’t be totally independent, they rely on producers for access and can’t consistently pan the product if they want to keep that. There’s a reason John Gilman isn’t tasting in Bordeaux any more. Also, the Wine Independent site itself, while it’s well written and well laid out, frankly reads like marketing copy for the producers being reviewed. I mean, come on.

CT is my go to source too. Clear #1. But with new releases that’s a bit harder and that’s why I rely on critics too. Some a bit more than others but never just on one critic. I like that there is a flurry of critics and I can read through 5 (Burgundy) or 15 (Bordeaux) notes and articles to get quite a good idea about a wine or vintage. Then add your own experience with a certain winery or type of vintage on top and you get a good picture of what to buy. CT can’t offer that for new releases.

A quick add-on to the “marketing copy” comment. She’s written about Yquem, Latour, Promontory and Dominus… of course the tone is rather optimistic. I suggest to judge the work, when we see full reports, not now.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
Those Fortune pieces and the whole “Wine Independent” rap have me rolling my eyes. There’s already a place to go for truly independent tasting notes - it’s called Cellartracker. People who work as professional wine critics can’t be totally independent, they rely on producers for access and can’t consistently pan the product if they want to keep that. There’s a reason John Gilman isn’t tasting in Bordeaux any more. Also, the Wine Independent site itself, while it’s well written and well laid out, frankly reads like marketing copy for the producers being reviewed. I mean, come on.

CT is my go to source too. Clear #1. But with new releases that’s a bit harder and that’s why I rely on critics too. Some a bit more than others but never just on one critic. I like that there is a flurry of critics and I can read through 5 (Burgundy) or 15 (Bordeaux) notes and articles to get quite a good idea about a wine or vintage. Then add your own experience with a certain winery or type of vintage on top and you get a good picture of what to buy. CT can’t offer that for new releases.

A quick add-on to the “marketing copy” comment. She’s written about Yquem, Latour, Promontory and Dominus… of course the tone is rather optimistic. I suggest to judge the work, when we see full reports, not now.

I agree with some of this but view it differently. On new releases, IMO there is rarely any reason to purchase before a wine is in bottle and has received reviews on CT and places like WB. First, in-barrel reviews are just not very reliable and are not tasting the same product you will receive in bottle. Second, with a few exceptions (2019?) you no longer save much if anything buying in EP from Bordeaux, and there is basically no scarcity reason to buy EP – except for a tiny number of ultra high end right bankers Bordeaux is never “sold out”. Same for Napa. So why not just wait until it’s in bottle and you can taste it yourself, and buy then? For Burgundy there are scarcity reasons to buy very early, but Burgundy critics are specialized and LPB is not one of them.

I do agree critics are useful to understand a vintage but you wouldn’t rely on a single critic to do that.

Critics play an educational role too of course, for wine lovers who are not as wonky or experienced as we are. And even for us, critics can be useful in turning us on to an obscure region. But it’s very particular critics who people come to really rely on to play that kind of role. I personally don’t see LPB as one of them based on her writing. My pre-conception of her is as kind of a not-pompous version of Suckling, in the sense that she mostly goes around bestowing high scores and extravagant adjectives on prestigious wines, except without a scarf or an affected accent. But perhaps she will emerge as something more now that she can spread her wings free of WA, who knows. The initial version of the web site continued to seem rather Suckling-esque to me, in the sense of celebrating high-end producers as a luxury product.

Hi Michael,

Well, I do my best to be down with the kids.

I notice you’re no slouch yourself at that, thinking a person of conviction cannot be anything other than a shill when their opinions do not align with your own free-thinking-do-my-own-research ideas.

Since you don’t know me very well I can assure you that the thoughts and opinions I express are ones that are not at all only my own. I share them with many, and most of them are nowhere near original. They have been mine and others spiel for the last decade. They just so happen to perhaps not having been aired too frequently inside this echo chamber.

Do you remember the days when you were able to listen to someone and say hmmm, hadn’t thought of that. And, hmmm, perhaps that person actually believes in something and has a personal opinion based on their experience.

Do you remember even just a couple of pages back in this thread when you expressed joy at being able to ignore whatever I might say? Ah, days of yesterrants.

To dismiss an argument which is based on pinpointable facts as mere selling seems a more zeitgeistish than anything I can think of. We’re living through a time in history when people recognize only agreement with their own notions as being authentic. Which is another non-original idea of mine.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
Johan, nice to see you so responsive on this thread. I noticed that your reports so far have coincided with releases through the Place de Bordeaux - is that the intention? Will you be doing big pieces contemporaneously with those releases to give them more publicity?

Thanks Greg,

We believe that timeliness is paramount.

Consumers crave reports on wines when they are actually available. And for those reviews to come as close to release as possible in order to make a decision based on as much information they can gather. An unavoidable side effect is that we then of course put a spotlight on the wines in question. It can be argued that it’s publicity if that’s how one choses to see it.

However, I would take the example of Latour 14. While we published on that wine we did not rave about it as being anything close to wine of the century. No hyperbolic statements. In my opinion a very good wine, but as I said in another thread, personally I’d rather have one bottle of 2016 Latour than 2 of 2014. For those who still want to pull the trigger at the price it’s being released I think they now know pretty well the quality of that wine and what to expect. A classic Pauillac.

It’s also worth remembering that these reports are very widely read and in demand. We can’t ignore that.

An unavoidable side effect is that we then of course put a spotlight on the wines in question. It can be argued that it’s publicity if that’s how one choses to see it.

However, I would take the example of Latour 14. While we published on that wine we did not rave about it as being anything close to wine of the century. No hyperbolic statements. In my opinion a very good wine, but as I said in another thread, personally I’d rather have one bottle of 2016 Latour than 2 of 2014. For those who still want to pull the trigger at the price it’s being released I think they now know pretty well the quality of that wine and what to expect. A classic Pauillac.

It’s also worth remembering that these reports are very widely read and in demand. We can’t ignore that.

Thanks Johan, I appreciate the point, but had you raved about 2014 Latour as the wine of the century you would have had fundamental issues - it’s not the best vintage. What would you say about great wine going forward!
My question was not intended to imply you would hype any wine released by the Place that week as the greatest. The scores/descriptions are there to be read. But I also hope you appreciate the inherent contradiction in the proclaimed goal of independence and the appearance of being Duclot’s marketing arm! Timeliness is important, but is anyone who’s interested in buying 2014 Latour or Yquem surprised by its release? These aren’t unicorns with small productions sold via allocations; these customers don’t need to make split second decisions. Nor, I’d have thought, would scores released earlier have harmed the process.

Cheers.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
I think LPB should hire Jonathan Newman, the guy who reviews all of the WTSO wines (and seems to have a 3-4 point rating scale, with everything rated 91-94). That’s the guy whose palate is trusted by millions.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
Those Fortune pieces and the whole “Wine Independent” rap have me rolling my eyes. There’s already a place to go for truly independent tasting notes - it’s called Cellartracker. People who work as professional wine critics can’t be totally independent, they rely on producers for access and can’t consistently pan the product if they want to keep that. There’s a reason John Gilman isn’t tasting in Bordeaux any more. Also, the Wine Independent site itself, while it’s well written and well laid out, frankly reads like marketing copy for the producers being reviewed. I mean, come on.

CT is my go to source too. Clear #1. But with new releases that’s a bit harder and that’s why I rely on critics too. Some a bit more than others but never just on one critic. I like that there is a flurry of critics and I can read through 5 (Burgundy) or 15 (Bordeaux) notes and articles to get quite a good idea about a wine or vintage. Then add your own experience with a certain winery or type of vintage on top and you get a good picture of what to buy. CT can’t offer that for new releases.

A quick add-on to the “marketing copy” comment. She’s written about Yquem, Latour, Promontory and Dominus… of course the tone is rather optimistic. I suggest to judge the work, when we see full reports, not now.

Cellar Tracker is a better source than all critics. They all rate too high. When you are tasting young wines and giving 98 points without decanting something is wrong here.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
Waiting to see if any duds receive sub-80 point scores and notes describing what’s wrong with them. To me, that would be a sign of a fiercely independent reviewer.

I would pay a $100 a year to see that!

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
I agree with some of this but view it differently. On new releases, IMO there is rarely any reason to purchase before a wine is in bottle and has received reviews on CT and places like WB. First, in-barrel reviews are just not very reliable and are not tasting the same product you will receive in bottle. Second, with a few exceptions (2019?) you no longer save much if anything buying in EP from Bordeaux, and there is basically no scarcity reason to buy EP – except for a tiny number of ultra high end right bankers Bordeaux is never “sold out”. Same for Napa. So why not just wait until it’s in bottle and you can taste it yourself, and buy then? For Burgundy there are scarcity reasons to buy very early, but Burgundy critics are specialized and LPB is not one of them.

Yes, with Bordeaux availability/scarcity is not an issue (for all other regions it’s an issue, maybe US for US is no issue either, but that’s not the case for US wines here in Europe). But re Bordeaux, I still buy EP (and need critics), for these 5 reasons:

A) You usually get at least 5-10%(ish) return from EP prices vs Arrivage prices (more in 2015 and 2019), not sexy (stock market would have most of the time performed better).
B) In every of the vintage there are a few hot wines which will cost you 25%, 35%, 50% more if you don’t bite from the first batch EP (think Canon 2015, Carmes HB 2016, Montrose 2016, Calon or Rauzan 2018). That hypes are driven by critics notes and scores.
C) In most Arrivage tastings I don’t find most high-end wines. Buying a bottle of each HB, Mission, Mouton, Lafite, Margaux, Cheval, Ausone, Trotanoy, etc. to see for yourself which of the big names did well and on which you’ll bet your money on is an expensive alternative way. Same is true for some hot wines which sold well or out EP and are hence not shown in Arrivage tastings
D) Large formats: will be infinity times harder to get for many names and most often cost you significantly more than EP.
E) Small Formats: hardly available after EP for many names

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
Cellar Tracker is a better source than all critics. They all rate too high. When you are tasting young wines and giving 98 points without decanting something is wrong here.

I don’t have a big problem with high ratings of certain critics as I’m probably less focused on points than you are. The context the articles and notes give are more important to me and I find a lot of information not in the absolute level of points a critic hands out but the relative one (ranking of the wines in a vintage, scores for a wine compared to scores of the same critic for the same wine in other vintages are much much more informative than the absolute level of a score, imo).

But for older vintages, CT is the best source. No question about that.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Yes, if you can’t ignore points completely, it’s sensible remind oneself that the scales are typically a 10 point rather than 100 point scale, and it’s done as a marketing trick to get people excited.

Perhaps find out what a critic’s average score is, then convert the rating into a + or - e.g. a Suckling 97 becomes a +1, and a 94 becomes a -2 if his average published score is 96.

I do find points a nonsense in matters of taste and preference though.

I am focused on points for sure but of course points are so inflated now a days. As mentioned many times before miss the ratings from Tanzer and Reynolds back in the day. Very accurate.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
Johan, nice to see you so responsive on this thread. I noticed that your reports so far have coincided with releases through the Place de Bordeaux - is that the intention? Will you be doing big pieces contemporaneously with those releases to give them more publicity?

Thanks Greg,

We believe that timeliness is paramount.

Consumers crave reports on wines when they are actually available. And for those reviews to come as close to release as possible in order to make a decision based on as much information they can gather. An unavoidable side effect is that we then of course put a spotlight on the wines in question. It can be argued that it’s publicity if that’s how one choses to see it.

However, I would take the example of Latour 14. While we published on that wine we did not rave about it as being anything close to wine of the century. No hyperbolic statements. In my opinion a very good wine, but as I said in another thread, personally I’d rather have one bottle of 2016 Latour than 2 of 2014. For those who still want to pull the trigger at the price it’s being released I think they now know pretty well the quality of that wine and what to expect. A classic Pauillac.

It’s also worth remembering that these reports are very widely read and in demand. We can’t ignore that.

Thanks Johan, I appreciate the point, but had you raved about 2014 Latour as the wine of the century you would have had fundamental issues - it’s not the best vintage. What would you say about great wine going forward!
My question was not intended to imply you would hype any wine released by the Place that week as the greatest. The scores/descriptions are there to be read. But I also hope you appreciate the inherent contradiction in the proclaimed goal of independence and the appearance of being Duclot’s marketing arm! Timeliness is important, but is anyone who’s interested in buying 2014 Latour or Yquem surprised by its release? These aren’t unicorns with small productions sold via allocations; these customers don’t need to make split second decisions. Nor, I’d have thought, would scores released earlier have harmed the process.

Cheers.

Thanks Greg,

I think the contradiction you point to is one which any critic of anything will always have to live by. I doubt releasing early scores would help. That could easily be seen as fuel for marketing and used accordingly to build expectations, prices and what have you.

While the wines are not unicorns for the most part, people still want to be informed. Subscribers want to feel they are in the loop and that there is a level playing field where there is no priority access for a select few paying exorbitant premiums. Whether they can actually use the information is rather not to the point and just a smoke screen argument for nothing-to-see-here crowd.

Split second decisions? Well, if they make them or not, subscribers want to feel that they can make them should the urge occur. That’s the privilege they pay for. Fair enough I think.

Cheers,
Johan

Johan,

I appreciate you taking the time to respond to me on a public forum. That said, I’m afraid I’m not following your reasoning here. Every reviewer I’m familiar with releases a large number of scores at once, once they are done with visits or larger tastings, appointments, etc. I have never seen anyone suggest that a release of scores in that manner is, in some way, fuel for the market or to build expectation. After all, scores must be released at some point. The argument you seem to be making is that since anyone can be accused of a conflict of interest, considering conflicts isn’t meaningful. I don’t think that’s true; I think, as has been discussed in various threads about wine criticisms over the years, the question is what you do to mitigate the conflict. Perhaps I have misunderstood?

As you say, I certainly sympathize with any consumer who wants to be informed about the wines they purchase and I don’t question the value to the consumer of having a score for a wine available the moment an offer for a wine hits their inbox. My question was specifically regarding the seeming incongruity of rather grand claims of independence while reviews appear to be published on the same schedule as the new releases from the Place de Bordeaux - and when those reviews are done in the glossy and high end style of an advertorial. For example, the Latour review states the wines were tasted in January, so publishing the review now to coincide with the wine’s release is a specific choice. To a somewhat more cynical observer, this does not suggest independence. If you were looking to provide consumers early information and put them on an equal footing, wouldn’t a review published earlier have been more efficient?

Best,

Greg

Happy to be here.

Not sure I get your conclusion on “The argument you seem to be making is that since anyone can be accused of a conflict of interest, considering conflicts isn’t meaningful”? I think considering conflicts is imperative and it is exactly what we’ve been communicating I believe.

However, “the question is what you do to mitigate the conflict” is pretty much exactly on point.

The Bordeaux machine is a very well oiled one and will be able to use reviews for their own purpose no matter when they are published I would propose. I believe that if you publish early or late will not matter so much to the final price of a wine. You can have your pick as a publisher. But, you also need to consider what you think your readers want. We believe that the timing we strive for is the right one.

Now, for glossy and advertorial. Well, that’s one way of looking at it. Another is that layout, design and photographs together with storytelling text can be appealing, beautiful and fun just for the appealing, beautiful and fun of it. Again, this style is one we believe will entice readership. Not because it’s an ad but rather because readers appreciate that sort of content. Not all will like it and so be it.

For us, none of the above are incongruous. We are transparent and the consumer can make an informed decision on our independence be they cynical curmudgeons or not. Grand indeed.

Transparency is something to nurture would you not agree?

posts.name: William Gladstone
posts.cooked:
I applaud the effort and action of this group with the aim to present a wine journal that is free of hidden commercial influences.

I am so fed up with the constant marketing efforts of an unnamed central wine publication to always solicit my financial participation in endless endeavors they are presenting.
The regular daily or every other day emails I receive from this publication has proven how far removed these owners are from simply wine journalism.

The high pressure tactics they employed on us to pay a higher membership fee because we utilize their reviews was merely a tip-off of what was to come in our relationship.
Where the other leading U.S. publications are helpful, cooperative and easy to work with, this major publication has made it so difficult to pass along information from the top
Chateaux we represent - that I just don’t want to bother contacting & updating this journal.

And now I will certainly attract much criticism - these are wine journals, including food reviews makes absolute sense, as we expect so often to pair the food with wine.
To then think they have license to give us their music reviews as a regular part of their journalism, and I have to get through their personal taste on music until I can find
the wine review really puts me off. I imagine that is merely a generational difference.

The writer is respected for his/her wine knowledge. Pairing with Food is a natural. To feel free to then think you have license to discourse on music is both an ego trip and
a lack of discipline.

I truly hope L.P. B. is successful enough that I can justify supporting her efforts and end my membership where I really do not want to be.
I am aware many folks will come after me that they enjoy the music reviews.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
I am so fed up with the constant marketing efforts of an unnamed central wine publication to always solicit my financial participation in endless endeavors they are presenting.
The regular daily or every other day emails I receive from this publication has proven how far removed these owners are from simply wine journalism.

All publications should stop with getting free samples. Anything beyond that is a conflict of interest.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Johan,

While I appreciate your willingness to respond, I must admit I’m quite puzzled by the responses themselves.

Happy to be here.

Not sure I get your conclusion on “The argument you seem to be making is that since anyone can be accused of a conflict of interest, considering conflicts isn’t meaningful”? I think considering conflicts is imperative and it is exactly what we’ve been communicating I believe.

However, “the question is what you do to mitigate the conflict” is pretty much exactly on point.

But Johan, you’ve not addressed how you’re mitigating the conflict. It appears to some of us that you’re acting in concert with the Place de Bordeaux and the negociants by releasing your reviews in step with their releases. I do not believe any other reviewing publication does this. Your response (which I address in more detail below) doesn’t actually address the conflict, it simply states the conflict is not relevant because Bordeaux is a well oiled machine. That is not how conflicts of interest are resolved! I could not, in my line of work, tell my conflicts team that the appearance of a conflict of interest was immaterial because the entire industry could use my work in any way, so it doesn’t matter.
For a publication that is trying to blaze a path on the basis of independence and lack of conflicts, that is a very strange response!

The Bordeaux machine is a very well oiled one and will be able to use reviews for their own purpose no matter when they are published I would propose. I believe that if you publish early or late will not matter so much to the final price of a wine. You can have your pick as a publisher. But, you also need to consider what you think your readers want. We believe that the timing we strive for is the right one.

I find this response to be completely backwards, to be honest. Your assertion here is that since the Bordeaux machine is well oiled, what you do in terms of publication is irrelevant, and what’s important is what YOU think the readers want. This strikes me as remarkably self serving! The argument is, so long as we give the readers what WE think they want, other issues are irrelevant. This reminds me very much of the Communist way of thinking - to give the masses what they need, we must first rule them. You’ll pardon the historical allusion, but that’s my undergraduate degree and emigre background talking. However, the principle is quite the same.
As far as I’m aware, yours is the only publication to propose this publication practice - surely you can see how that leaves you more open for the need for an explanation of how you are maintaining your independence, not a blithe statement that “its what the readers want”.

Now, for glossy and advertorial. Well, that’s one way of looking at it. Another is that layout, design and photographs together with storytelling text can be appealing, beautiful and fun just for the appealing, beautiful and fun of it. Again, this style is one we believe will entice readership. Not because it’s an ad but rather because readers appreciate that sort of content. Not all will like it and so be it.

For us, none of the above are incongruous. We are transparent and the consumer can make an informed decision on our independence be they cynical curmudgeons or not. Grand indeed.

Transparency is something to nurture would you not agree?

I strongly agree Johan! Transparency in the world of wine journalism is wonderful. On the subject of transparency, are you disclosing that these reviews are timed with the releases at the Place de Bordeaux? Also, are you accepting free lodging or invitations to special dinners in Bordeaux?

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
Johan,

While I appreciate your willingness to respond, I must admit I’m quite puzzled by the responses themselves.





Well, the response I believe is what I’ve said a couple of times over, that conflict is no more or less present if you publish early or late. I cannot state it clearer than that. It’s that simple. In fact you will find that the publications on Latour and Yquem are timed within an extremely narrow time frame of the releases if you look at Vinous and WA.

William Kelley for example released his notes on the very day of release for Latour if I’m not mistaken, stating expressly that the review was being published because of the release on the Place de Bordeaux.

So I think you need to examine this a bit closer.

And I think that what you see as a conflict of interest is something I do not recognize as such.

If you want to have a world in which reviews exist, well, then the perception of conflict of interest which bothers you will always exist also. If you read a review on wine, food, music, film, cars, books… anything… 99.99% of the time the product reviewed will also be for sale. And yes, the commercial actors in each industry will use them for financial gain. It simply is an unavoidable fact. Almost every review in every field is timed so that the readers will enjoy them the most. TWI is not an outlier of any sort in this respect. And especially not in the field of wine review publications. I’m unclear on how this is not apparent?

If the by you perceived conflict of interest needs a solution then that solution is to stop reviews from happening. There is no other way. Had there been, then the world of reviewing would have found it and it would be present in the market place. It is not.

As for self-serving, well that is of course one way of looking at it. We are a publication, and we want to keep our readers happy. Not sure if I think that is much of a scoop. We believe our approach serves them well and we serve at their pleasure, not the other way round. Again, applicable to any other publication you care to mention.

Communism, hmmm, the word stretch comes to mind…

On transparency, my favourite word. Again as stated above and previously, as for timing, in the articles themselves we say they are timed with the release so I’m unsure why you ask? And again, this is common practice I would say. If you do not see it other places then I am confused because it is overt and clearly stated often by all. If you believe we are in concert with the Place de Bordeaux then you will have to say the same of all wine reviewers as this is a clear fact.

We have also always been clear on the idea that we will not become hermits and stop living life. I’ve addressed this before. We will socialize and sit down for dinners and share meals. We always bring a bottle of significance as a contribution. This is part of being human and enjoying wine and life. If that makes you doubt our independence, then you are free to do so.

That’s the exact point, with TWI you will be able to take at face value our behaviour and you will not have to guess. Everyone has a line where they think influence becomes an improper factor. If you believe that a reviewer needs to stop living a normal life to be trusted, then that is your prerogative. Asceticism is just not what we consider something to strive for.

I’ve said it before, when you read a review on Margaux 1900, Latour 1961, Mouton 1945 or any given DRC, from a reviewer of substance, most likely they were free and presented in a social setting. If you want to read such reviews that is simply something you will have to come to terms with.

Now it’s up to you to decide if our practices are comparable to others. Do you feel that you have the same information from others?

I appreciate your discussion and line of thought but am a little disoriented by some conclusions.

As with some of your responses below, you present an all or nothing approach. Since reviews are released to be somewhat connected to the release of the wine, examining their timing is no longer necessary. That’s not a realistic answer.

And again, this is common practice I would say. If you do not see it other places then I am confused because it is overt and clearly stated often by all. We always bring a bottle of significance as a contribution. This is part of being human and enjoying wine and life. If that makes you doubt our independence, then you are free to do so.

While I’ve found some of our discussion above interesting, this answer was arresting. No wine reviewer is expected to take up the habit of a Franciscan monk, but there is a gap between those two poles big enough to pilot a container ship through without it getting stuck. The question is not whether you enjoy a nice dinner but whether you are being invited to special dinners or receiving free wine from the estates and domaines that you review. That you enjoy life (which I certainly hope you do!) is entirely beside the point! For a publication with the word INDEPENDENT in its name, I hope you can see how such a response is troubling! How is “don’t worry, I also bring a nice bottle” a satisfactory mitigation of conflicts of interest when attending a dinner at Lafite where they supply all the other wines?

That’s the exact point, with TWI you will be able to take at face value our behaviour and you will not have to guess. Everyone has a line where they think influence becomes an improper factor. If you believe that a reviewer needs to stop living a normal life to be trusted, then that is your prerogative. Asceticism is just not what we consider something to strive for.

But I can’t take your behavior at face value! I’ve asked you a direct question - do you accept invitations to special dinners - and your answer was that you will continue to enjoy life. I have no idea what that means - how am I meant to determine your independence?

I’ve said it before, when you read a review on Margaux 1900, Latour 1961, Mouton 1945 or any given DRC, from a reviewer of substance, most likely they were free and presented in a social setting. If you want to read such reviews that is simply something you will have to come to terms with.

Publications like the Wine Advocate, Jeff Leve, John Gilman, etc., make the sourcing of their wines quite clear. My understanding is that other than barrel samples, they pay for their wines or drink them in private settings without the producers. (Fellow board members, please correct me if I’m wrong.) That is quite clear to me. Your practices, I’m afraid, are not. I asked a specific question - would you be so kind as to provide a specific response? It was not a trick question!

You also refer repeatedly to other publications - I appreciate that different wine reviewers have different policies, but “they do X” isn’t an answer to questions about transparency.

Now it’s up to you to decide if our practices are comparable to others. Do you feel that you have the same information from others?

But Johan, to be honest, I feel like I have NO information from you at all! I have asked two questions. First, whether the timing of releases is something you perceive as an issue, which you simply dismissed as not a conflict. I am somewhat dismayed by this, but OK. As to my second specific question, you simply didn’t address it and insisted you will not be held to a asceticism, something no one has asked. I am quite puzzled - what exactly does independence mean here? I think it would be really helpful if we could establish some information. To aid us, could tell us:

  • Do you accept free housing during your visits?
    Do you accept free housing when in wine regions when you are not doing tastings?
    Do you bring family with you on these trips?
    Do you attend special dinners which are not disclosed to the readership?
    Do you charge estates to use your scores?

I think all this information would be helpful in terms of transparency and independence. I certainly think one can enjoy life and not be a hermit without doing any of the above! (While no estate has yet offered to pay me to travel to Bordeaux and use my scores, it has not forced me to live in a cave.)

I appreciate your discussion and line of thought but am a little disoriented by some conclusions.

On this, Johan, I believe we are entirely in agreement. I’m really looking forward to your answers Johan, I think it’s critical for a publication named The Wine Independent to establish its valid independence bona fides right off the start, don’t you?

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: You need to further specify your third question for it to be apt. Do you have family trips paid for by anyone but you or your publication? I’ve brought my wife along on business trips (once when I gave a paper in Nice, for instance), but we paid her way.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: I can’t speak for Greg, so I’ll speak for myself. The question about dinners isn’t about in whose company you dine, but about the auspices under which the dinner is held. Are the events limited to winemakers and reviewers? Are they paid for by the wineries. I would distinguish that from being taken out by a winemaker or owner who is your friend, though, in that case, I would consider that reviewing that person’s wine might be a conflict of interest. I get the feeling that many wine critics feel it is part of their job to be friendly with winemakers and domaine owners. To the extent that it is true, it is a problem endemic to the industry. You would not find the situation among film, theater and, for the most part, book reviewers for reputable publications. But this issue is different from any Greg has raised.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
I can’t speak for Greg, so I’ll speak for myself. The question about dinners isn’t about in whose company you dine, but about the auspices under which the dinner is held. Are the events limited to winemakers and reviewers? Are they paid for by the wineries. I would distinguish that from being taken out by a winemaker or owner who is your friend, though, in that case, I would consider that reviewing that person’s wine might be a conflict of interest. I get the feeling that many wine critics feel it is part of their job to be friendly with winemakers and domaine owners. To the extent that it is true, it is a problem endemic to the industry. You are completely correct in assuming that critics consider it to be part of the job. I’ve spent time with my fair share of them and and I agree that it is, and in fact both should and needs to be, part of the job. This is how stories get uncovered and meaningful conversations beyond the marketing rap happen. It is intrinsic to uncovering truths. If that is transparent then the consumer is free to decide if it constitutes an infringement on independence or not. Fair enough if they do. But also fair enough if they think it is of usefulness.

posts.cooked:
I can’t speak for Greg, so I’ll speak for myself. The question about dinners isn’t about in whose company you dine, but about the auspices under which the dinner is held. Are the events limited to winemakers and reviewers? Are they paid for by the wineries. I would distinguish that from being taken out by a winemaker or owner who is your friend, though, in that case, I would consider that reviewing that person’s wine might be a conflict of interest. I get the feeling that many wine critics feel it is part of their job to be friendly with winemakers and domaine owners. To the extent that it is true, it is a problem endemic to the industry. You would not find the situation among film, theater and, for the most part, book reviewers for reputable publications. But this issue is different from any Greg has raised.

You are completely correct in assuming that critics consider it to be part of the job. I’ve spent time with my fair share of them and and I agree that it is, and in fact both should and needs to be, part of the job. This is how stories get uncovered and meaningful conversations beyond the marketing rap happen. It is intrinsic to uncovering truths. If that is transparent then the consumer is free to decide if it constitutes an infringement on independence or not. Fair enough if they do. But also fair enough if they think it is of usefulness.

Using the normal definition of conflict of interest, which certainly covers not reviewing the work of one’s friends, all this means is that conflict of interest is built into wine reviewing. Transparency means that you admit and live with the conflict of interest, not that it isn’t there, though for transparency to be complete, the reader would need to know precisely your relationship with each winemaker’s or property owner’s wine you review. This, by the way, is hardly out of the norm for almost every other form of journalism. In news stories in the New York Times, and the Washington Post, connections between the newspapers and/or the authors of stories and their content is scrupulously revealed. The Washington Post mentions that they are owned by Jeff Bezos so regularly that my eyes have started to glaze over.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
I think you miss a distinction I was making. It’s one thing to say that conflict of interest is OK if it is coupled with complete transparency. But whether there is such transparency is another issue. For that to exist, you would have to declare all possible conflicts with each review in each review. I am aware that wine publications do not generally do this. That is a fault of the guild and not an indemnification of your publication.

Nor was it always so. When Parker began and billed himself as the Ralph Nader of wine critics, he was contrasting himself all UK critics, who he felt were too much in bed with the owners, winemakers, importers, etc. At that time, he personally bought the wine he reviewed. Some of that corde sanitaire eroded of necessity in order to review futures and wine before it arrived in the US. More eroded as he got more comfortable knowing and being friendly with people in the industry. More eroded from failure to do due diligence. Now, by your testimony, there is nothing left of it. That may be the state of the case, but as Parker’s start and the situation of critics who do not have sufficient weight to get favored treatment shows, it does not have to be the case.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
Troubling as it may be I gotta say Greg, I am sorry that it somehow does not seem to translate, but I have in fact answered all your questions as you’ve stated them already. It’s been invigorating and I thank you for it but it’s all become a bit circular at this point.

While the answers may not be sufficient for your approval, they are still there in the lengthy posts above. Anyone still reading this can look my answers over and make their minds up as they please on whether I am ducking out or not. That might certainly be your take. But as I read the posts over again I see that they express exactly what I intend and that they are not opaque in any way unless that is how one wishes to read them.

Our solution to inherent issues is what it is. You find contradiction where there is none by nitpicking and inferring, and I am convinced that all we are doing at this stage is rehashing.

That may be the greatest non-answer I’ve seen on such a subject - it is the equivalent of a shrug! I’m extremely surprised that for a publication that calls itself The Wine Independent you don’t have a rigid set of independence standards that you can immediately provide for us without having to resort to comparisons of other reviewers, vague industry practices or dismissal of clear issues as non-conflicts. When asked point blank about dinners paid for by chateaux you bristled that I demand you live like a hermit. Yet I made no such demand!

For the record, I found few contradictions in your posts and I agree you expressed what you intended - which is very little other than “people are free to read us or not”. That is true, but it doesn’t go any of the substance. It is what it, Johan. Which, in this case, may well be rife with hidden conflict. Who’s to know?
