posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
shrug

Again Greg, that is sometimes all that remains when someone does not like the answers they’ve been given. What can one do other than shrug?

Anyone interested can go back in this thread and see the answers given and that they specifically address your points.

Your idea of a line in the sand is your personal one to have. Fair enough. We’ve been more than clear on where our line is. Since you think that line is too far you do not accept my answers. No Bristle involved. That’s all in your perception.

We believe in having dinners with producers because, and I repeat, that is where life is, that is where stories are, that is where secrets get told, that is where unusual wines are shared. If the reader wants that sort of information then that is the trade off. We do not believe it impacts independence and we are transparent about that. You will not find another publication with a different opinion. Any critic which visits Bordeaux is doing this because it is integral to the work.

Our line in the sand is drawn there.

We do not want to charge producers money for tables or other things or have premium preview subscriptions. You may call this vague but it is not. It is an integral part of the industry, hidden from the majority of subscribers, and for us the major problem. We believe the consumer has a right to know. As a member of this board you would have to be blind not to have actual knowledge of these things. Vague is not an apt description of these practices when it comes to Berserkers.

You are looking for a smoking gun where there is none. The gun is in plain sight and you and I, and everyone else who has not deleted this thread, see it. For you to discount that “our solution is what it is” and calling it a shrug after I’ve been painstakingly clear is rather ungenerous. You may not accept it as being enough, which is certainly your right, but to say that it is not being honest is just slightly unfair.

And yes, it is what it is in the end. If our line in the sand is one which people can not stomach and in fact do find ripe with conflict well then that is where matters stand. We believe that transparency is the best solution. I accept that some will not think that is enough. Then again, that means that all wine reviewing of actual substance and interest is off the table. Which is fine if that is your preference.

As for shrugging, I believe my engagement here on the forum, and with you in particular, is anything but.

posts.name: Miran Kegl
posts.cooked:
Johan, I must give it to you - you seem to have enough stamina to feed even the most ardent trolls. Personally, I would become completely exhausted and question why I’m waisting my time. A classic problem with trolls is that the more you feed them, the more you encourage them. I’m afraid that sooner or later you’ll stumble upon someone with serious OCD tendencies, who have all the time in the world to engage you in conversation, no matter what and how you reply. You’ll be stuck in a perpetual tango motion.

I for one, applaud your initiative (not without some criticism), mainly because I feel that any endeavour to spread the passion for wine should be commended. We need more passion in this world, not less. When I read through the comments in your thread, I notice there are people with a sincere curiosity about what you are doing and what you are about to launch. I also notice people who have honest concerns about some issues and rightly so. And then… Well, then you have people who have absolutely NO interest in your project, no matter how good or bad - they are the ones who just feed on stirring the pot by poking some negativity into whatever comes across their path. The situation reminds me very much of a radio program, Hassan, that know you will be familiar with as a Swede but that will unfortunately be difficult to convey to others. I’ll try my best to offer a translation.

- Bamsegrill, Bogdan! (Bamsegrill is the name of a small kiosk that sells sausages, snacks and miscellaneous fast food, Bogdan is the name of the person answering (and working there).
_- Hello, is this the snack bar?

  • This is Bamsegrill, yes.
  • Hello, I’m calling from Radio Sweden._ (the Swedish national radio broadcaster) _Do you have home-made mashed potatoes?
  • Yes, we do mashed potatoes from potatoes.
  • Hmm, is it home-made?
  • Yes, it’s home-made.
  • How do you transport it between your home and the kiosk?
  • Yes, well, we make it in the kiosk.
  • Do you LIVE in the kiosk?
  • No, we live at home.
  • Yes, well then it can hardly be “home-made”? I asked you if you have home-made mashed potatoes.
  • Yes-yes, well in that case it’s not “home-made”. Then it’s “kiosk-made”. Can I help you with anything else?
  • No, that was the only thing I wanted to know.
  • We also have grilled sausages.
  • Thank you! Bye!
  • Bye._

It’s epic. A classic. And so eloquently showing the great “diversity” of people in the world - that you will have some members of our species who will actually spend their time and thoughts to check out the kiosk, although they have no interest whatsoever in purchasing any products from that very establishment, nor wish to have any dealings with them at all. They’re just calling to make sure you’re not living in the kiosk. God bless them…

I would like to add one comment and one concern (or call it criticism).

I’ll start with the latter. In my humble opinion, there are NO wine publications out there who are “independent” (I won’t get philosophical here by spending time on elabourous definitions - I think most people will understand the word). I know there are publications that would like to THINK they are independent, like one which at this very minute is gathering some famous Barolo winemakers in New York and who charges $2000 per month from retailers (!) to receive a three days advance notice about the score (that we, the subscribers fund them to collect through our paid subscription). How’s that for true independence? And this is just one of countless examples of a wine world where there is no true “independence”, no matter how you look at it. Wine reviewers have connections, they have friends within the wine community, they have access to special tastings and dinners. Nothing new under the sky. They are all influenced by these connections, one way or another, directly or indirectly - I think it’s the way they respond to this influence that matters. There are probably wine critics who rate wines more harshly from winemakers they know, as a way to distance them from any positive influence. And I’m sure there are wine critics who shell out higher scores because they have been treated to so much generosity from the winemakers. It’s human nature, and nothing to really get upset by.

However, here I completely agree with you, Johan. At the end of the day a consumer of your product, a subscription to your journal where you give advice on wines that you should run your legs off to aquire, is based on one thing only - trust. Trust in your ability to taste and recognise quality - or occasional shout out about fantastic deals based on the low price and high quality ratio. That’s the only thing I’m interested in, as a paying subscriber. The day I feel you are giving bad advice or score wines highly just because you happen to socialise with the winemaker or because you had a wonderful dinner in your honour, I will have no use for you. Count me out - I just want good, insider’s advice on wines that will benefit ME. Whether you are “independent” or not. For all care, you can run the Marathon du Médoc completely naked or have private sex parties with the winemakers - I don’t really care as long as you provide the best advice and impressions possible, that will help me finding gems that would otherwise not have come to my attention.

Sure, there will be people arguing that no-no, they really want to take advice from TRULY independent sources of information. If THAT’S their main objective, much more so than receiving good advice from people with passion and knowledge about wine, and who might have a similar palate to your own, I know a Japanese guy in the jungle’s of Burma. His name is Jungle Jim Hirohito-san and is an ex-soldier who has fled the turmoils of modern world, completely isolated himself and now lives in a makeshift shed in the middle of the steamy rainforest. He knows absolutely NOTHING about wine, has no connections whatsoever but…he is 100% independent. If you would give him a sip to taste, he’d tell you instantly what he thinks about it. Question now is…what are my main objective? The independence of the person I take advice from (he’s the real deal when it comes to that), or the best advice possible from a person who is indeed not independed but rather is exposed to all sorts of potential conflicts of interest? I think I’ll go for the latter, although the former, Jungle Jim, is quite a nice guy - you’d like him. I can only compare to my own experiences. Had I been a professional wine critic, I would set up a visit to, for example, Dönnhoff and make it a one hour visit where the first minute is a cordial “hello” and the remaining time is spent in silence tasting, spitting and scribbling down impressions, until time is up and you say goodbye. In fact, one could argue that simply by showing up you expose yourself to becoming influenced by the winemaker. Perhaps the most neutral thing would be to just have the wines sent to you and then drinking them blind with just a tag number on the bottle, that will only be revealed after you have tasted the wines (or even until you have published the tasting notes). Now THAT would be true independence - of course - you would also pay for the samples and the shipment. I do nothing of the sort - instead I spend hours and hours tasting with Helmut Dönnhoff and mix discussions about the wines tasted with stories about history, memories, climate, soil, vineyard management and all sorts of background stories that has led up to where the Weingut is today. And from this, I obtain an immensely better knowledge and understanding about their wines than even most professionals do. I would actually rather take advice from someone like myself then some kind of self-appointed “independent”.

My criticism is limited to the unfortunate use of “Independent” in the name you have chosen. Based on my opinion above, that there ARE NO true independent wine publications out there. When you use it as a company name you expose yourself to ridicule and questioning and I think that’s exactly what is triggering some people to engage you in troll-like behaviour. If there’s a potential balloon to pop, they’ll gladly try to make it burst. Very much similar to the equally unfortunate concept and phrasing of “natural wines” - this too creates animosity (and rightly so!) because people get pissed-off by the fact that someone believes they are “natural” while others are not. I’m not your investor so I have no say but had I been, I would opt for a different name for the publication. You could still make bad-ass articles and tastings that will blow others out of the water, without the animosity caused by the name.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked: Why are people asking skeptical but reasonable questions in this thread “trolls”, while the person relentlessly flogging a commercial product that he is personally heavily invested in isn’t? I mean, I appreciate Johan answering questions here, but his incentive is clear

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
Johan, I must give it to you - you seem to have enough stamina to feed even the most ardent trolls. Personally, I would become completely exhausted and question why I’m waisting my time. A classic problem with trolls is that the more you feed them, the more you encourage them. I’m afraid that sooner or later you’ll stumble upon someone with serious OCD tendencies, who have all the time in the world to engage you in conversation, no matter what and how you reply. You’ll be stuck in a perpetual tango motion.

I for one, applaud your initiative (not without some criticism)…

Well, what can I say Miran… Thanks comes to mind.

I believe you have pretty much described our efforts in a crystalline way and I will have to refer to this post many times in the future I suspect.

Your concern/criticism is one which Lisa and I have come to terms with and will defend until the end.

We believe that the name we have chosen is strong, powerful, and instantly clear. Not all will agree, and some will make the points you suggest. For them, the name is poorly chosen. They will take offence, be triggered, and try to counter argue. They are a minority and most likely were never going to be subscribers in any case.

We have weighed the pros and cons and ultimately there is no pleasing everybody.

What I can tell you is that the reception of the name has been one of almost unanimous acclaim outside a small world of a few upset critics and perhaps a phalanx in these pages. We have enjoyed support from amateurs and professionals alike.

Even in the world of critics you would be more than a little surprised with the amount of personal communications of encouragement coming our way.

So, no regrets.

The name is TWI and it’s as perfect one as we could have ever dreamed up. Well, except for maybe THI (The Helicopter Independent). But since we have none with that particular skillset on our team we thought it too far flung, pun intended. You should run out and trademark it asap.

Cheers,

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Why are people asking skeptical but reasonable questions in this thread “trolls”, while the person relentlessly flogging a commercial product that he is personally heavily invested in isn’t? I mean, I appreciate Johan answering questions here, but his incentive is clear

Troll is a label and labels can be dangerous because we’ve already got in our mind what that term means (and that may vary between us). To be fair the word spam is similar.

I applaud Johan for continuing to politely respond here, to what I think are mostly (but perhaps not entirely) reasonable challenges.
I do encourage him to not use this thread or forum as a marketing vehicle to advertise their latest features / offers. Unsolicited advertising is generally not popular on chat forums.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
My criticism is limited to the unfortunate use of “Independent” in the name you have chosen. Based on my opinion above, that there ARE NO true independent wine publications out there. When you use it as a company name you expose yourself to ridicule and questioning and I think that’s exactly what is triggering some people to engage you in troll-like behaviour. If there’s a potential balloon to pop, they’ll gladly try to make it burst. Very much similar to the equally unfortunate concept and phrasing of “natural wines” - this too creates animosity (and rightly so!) because people get pissed-off by the fact that someone believes they are “natural” while others are not. I’m not your investor so I have no say but had I been, I would opt for a different name for the publication. You could still make bad-ass articles and tastings that will blow others out of the water, without the animosity caused by the name.

That is a curious reading of the situation Johan, since it is clearly not an unfortunate choice but rather both the cri de couer and raison d’etre of the entire effort. From the initial press release:

Lisa Perotti-Brown's press release:

I don’t want to point fingers at anyone else’s business in particular, but people don’t know what’s happening behind the scenes of so many wine criticism publications, unless you’re in the business, and then you are like, ‘oh my God’.”

“A consumer reading a publication that tells you what you should and shouldn’t buy has no idea there are other agendas behind the scenes, so we are saying we are independent in the name, there is never going to be any wriggle room on that, there is never going to be any compromise on integrity, and that comes with us [Perotti-Brown and Berglund] being majority shareholders, and the others being very much in agreement with our ethos – no-one is going to get greedy; we want to remain completely unbiased.”

the new publication is said to “return to the high ethical standards initially championed by Robert M. Parker, Jr. back when he started The Wine Advocate in 1978,” noting that “a reputable wine criticism publication should not accept money from any other wine-related entities” …

This publication was started with both a broadside against the entire industry as being hopelessly compromised and a claim that they would be completely independent. Johan has now, on this thread, argued that he will accept money from the Chateaux for dinners because “he will not stop enjoying life”, will charge them for scores and has deflected the other questions. Now, to the extent other publications want to operate in that manner, that’s their business - but they are not on this site arguing that they are not just independent, but more independent than anyone else.

And, to be clear, it is also Johan’s business if he wants to do that. But, if TWI wants to be treated like Caesar’s wife, they can’t also enjoy an occasional fling. Like having their Lafite and drinking it too.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Well, this has certainly been interesting Johan. I admit, I am unfamiliar with your work as a photographer, but you certainly have a way with words. As someone who’s paid to read and write precisely for a living, I genuinely appreciate your skill with obfuscation, elision and implication - you have worked very hard to refuse to answer my questions and instead imply that I am unhappy with your answers. From a professional perspective, I rather admire it! From a personal perspective - well, it’s a good thing I’m a cheerful person by nature. Otherwise I imagine I’d be upset at you repeatedly mischaracterizing my position in the service of advertising your publication on this site.

Anyway, I’d love to get some answers from you given all your lofty claims of transparency and independence. That is, after all, why you’ve started this publication!

Anyone interested can go back in this thread and see the answers given and that they specifically address your points.

Surely, Johan, answering my questions again, given that we’ve all been kind enough to give you space to advertise your publication should not be so hard? I restate them below. I repeat, you have not answered these. You’ve said you have, but you’ve not.

  • Do you accept free housing during your visits?
    Do you accept free housing when in wine regions when you are not doing tastings?
    Do you bring family with you on these trips (for which you do not pay separately)?
    Do you attend special dinners which are not disclosed to the readership (again, please note the distinction between special events put on by chateaux and dinners with producers - these aren’t the same)?
    Do you charge estates to use your scores? (At least to this you’ve appeared to answer yes.)
    How much of the above do you disclose to your readers?


Your idea of a line in the sand is your personal one to have.

Once again, I’ve presented no line, sand or otherwise. You have said this several times, but this is just flat out untrue. I’d like to understand your standard of behavior. You’ve refused to provide one.

We believe in having dinners with producers because, and I repeat, that is where life is, that is where stories are, that is where secrets get told, that is where unusual wines are shared. If the reader wants that sort of information then that is the trade off. We do not believe it impacts independence and we are transparent about that. You will not find another publication with a different opinion. Any critic which visits Bordeaux is doing this because it is integral to the work.

Our line in the sand is drawn there.

That is not a line in the sand. That’s just a policy about enjoying dinners. Which, sure - I enjoy having dinners with producers too! But, of course, that was not my question. Nor did I suggest you shouldn’t have dinners with producers. You’ve invented that out of whole cloth.
My question, and I will be specific, is whether you attend special dinners put on by chateaux where they supply all the wines. Also, whether you disclose that to your readership. To the extent you have dinner with the winemaker at Lafite who brings a bottle - that is wonderful! I hope you enjoy it! I hope he has wonderful stories. I do rather hope you see the difference between that and being served 10 bottles of out Lafite’s cellar at a fancy dinner at the estate for which you do not pay. There is a world of difference between the two. I don’t think you need a gala event at Latour to learn their secrets, wouldn’t you agree? Especially since, you rate their wines and charge them for ratings! If I tried to make my conflict of interest policy at work “I will not stop receiving kick backs, this is how I enjoy life and that is how my clients give me inside information”, I suspect my conflicts team (and various legal organizations) may have some concerns!

We do not want to charge producers money for tables or other things or have premium preview subscriptions. You may call this vague but it is not. It is an integral part of the industry, hidden from the majority of subscribers, and for us the major problem. We believe the consumer has a right to know. As a member of this board you would have to be blind not to have actual knowledge of these things. Vague is not an apt description of these practices when it comes to Berserkers.

I have no idea why you write of vagueness here, but I asked a simple question - do you charge producers money to use your scores? Your answer is, from what I can tell of the verbal morass above, is yes. You’re trying to qualify it by writing some unrelated comments about how you don’t charge people hidden fees, but you DO charge producers for use of your scores. You’ve written this is for copyright but, of course, that’s an elision - you do not have to enforce that.
Since we are all now agreed you do charge producers for use of your scores, can you explain how you maintain your independence despite this? Your press release (on the first page of this thread) made claims of being more independent than other publications. Yet many reviewers do not charge to use their scores at all. You do. How is independence then maintained? Please do not refer to “industry standard” - you’ve made your pitch by claiming to be more independent. If you’re no more independent than others, that rather weakens the grand claims, doesn’t it?
And please, spare me the “line in the sand” nonsense, I haven’t actually said this makes you fatefully compromised. I’m trying to get you to answer what your actual policies are. You charge for ratings, how do you mitigate the inherent conflict of interest and independence?

And yes, it is what it is in the end. If our line in the sand is one which people can not stomach and in fact do find ripe with conflict well then that is where matters stand. We believe that transparency is the best solution. I accept that some will not think that is enough. Then again, that means that all wine reviewing of actual substance and interest is off the table. Which is fine if that is your preference.

I agree, transparency is the best solution! I wish you’d practice it on this thread though. It does not inspire confidence going forward.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
I genuinely appreciate your skill with obfuscation, elision and implication

Thanks Greg. I aim to please. In return, I appreciate your skill at not being able to recognize answers. You seem to have taken a masterclass. As per usual at this point, I invite anyone to just go over the posts. The answers are simple, I’ve given them, you do not like them… and repeat.

I think perhaps you have an alter ego as one of the script writers for the much beloved Hassan group which Miran was referring to.

posts.name: Bruce G.
posts.cooked:
For you to discount that “our solution is what it is” and calling it a shrug after I’ve been painstakingly clear is rather ungenerous. You may not accept it as being enough, which is certainly your right, but to say that it is not being honest is just slightly unfair.

And yes, it is what it is in the end. If our line in the sand is one which people can not stomach and in fact do find ripe with conflict well then that is where matters stand. We believe that transparency is the best solution. I accept that some will not think that is enough. Then again, that means that all wine reviewing of actual substance and interest is off the table. Which is fine if that is your preference.

Johan:

Going on the assumption that the true purpose of the internet is for people to give free, unsolicited advice to others about their business models, here is my contribution.

  1. Write it down…
    The OP has Lisa stating that TWI will be uncompromising on integrity. While those are welcome words to anyone who is looking for credible, unbiased buying advice, without a substantive detailing of how that will be achieved it can seem little more than platitudes and marketing talk. I think it would be wise to provide a highly visible link on your home page to a formal declaration of the practices you plan on adopting in your pursuit of uncompromised integrity. It should be as detailed and specific as possible, outlining what you will and what you will not do as wine critics.
    Of course, it would be impossible to address every hypothetical instance of ethics pertaining to wine criticism. But many of the possible “traps” can be imagined.
    Having a policy statement out there, accessible to all, and subject to revision whenever the need arises, would be a very good thing.

  2. Seek outside oversight…
    I know it sounds troublesome (and somewhat costly), but I think you might want to hire an independent ombudsman who will periodically audit your practices to make sure they maintain the integrity laid out in the policy statement mentioned above. This person would have full access to what you do, and could issue periodic reports. And they could serve as a conduit whenever a subscriber (or other interested party) has questions/claims about TWI’s ethical stance.
    It would also save you and Lisa the headache of wading in to each and every discussion of the “I heard some guy say that…” type.

I can imagine that this might come across as too much.
But merely saying “we will be uncomproming in our pursuit of integrity and we won’t do what the other guys do” will probably not allay the concerns of an undertandably skeptical audience.
Nor would it be breaking new ground in the field.

Best of luck to you in your venture…

Thanks Bruce,

Happy to see another G join. Just hoping the more famous Kenny will also jump in.

To your first point.
You will be happy to hear that what you seek is naturally part of the site when it launches.

To your second point.
This would be of little use I’m afraid. That person would just spend most of their days rehashing and placating the unplacateable There will always be those who think what we do will not be independent enough. You will always and forever get “I heard some guy say that…”. So many internet types thinking “Ah, got ya!”. When in fact, all they got, is being personally overexcited.

It’s simple as it ever was:

Over and over I’ve stated that at TWI we will join in dinners and tastings when there is a benefit to be had for finding information, tasting notes, stories, photographs or exposure of our message and communication. We will live a normal life in this aspect and not a a gravy train one. Industry standard, and not one we think is problematic when transparency is adopted. Those worried about this are free to feel so. Should they find us disingenuous that is their right and fair enough. Some have standards differing from ours and that’s ok.

We will not participate in the practice of charging money from producers at events. This is now an industry standard for the major players. Transparency, not so much

We will not sell ultra premium subscriptions be they preview or what have you. Something at the moment only present in a couple of places, but examined closely as an avenue by others who pointedly refuse to take a stand against it. As you may have noticed or not. Transparency certainly not applied.

To use a favourite expression of another G in the thread, that is our line in the sand. Consumers can have their choice on wether it is drawn correctly. Consumers can decide on if it makes a difference for them or not. Consumers can put their trust where they see fit. Face value.

I do hope Kenny will be a subscriber! After, all I’ve got a signed saxophone of his hanging over the fireplace.

posts.cooked:
“To your second point.
This would be of little use I’m afraid. That person would just spend most of their days rehashing and placating the unplacateable There will always be those who think what we do will not be independent enough. You seem not to know that the word “ombudsman” is actually a swedish word, since this is where it was invented. Comes from the ancient “umbuðsmann”. A word that’s been around for more than 800 years. Not sure when you Anglos adopted it? Most Swedes know this and 10 min of research would be needed for very few of them.

You also seem to misunderstand my meaning and I apologize if it was at all unclear. The internet types I refer to are the ones which our ombudsman would have to deal with taking into consideration. To investigate and solve the complaints of your typical Greg K is as an exhausting and fruitless endeavour as one could possibly hope to find.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: I have an OED and I know the etymology. But ombudsmen are not obligated to respond to any internet troll or, indeed, and complaint in particular. They are there to represent complaints that they, in their role as ombudsmen, think deserve representation. Having officially employed outside adjudicators for editorial and business practices is really not uncommon. All it means is that you employ someone to look over your shoulder at your practices. Your response about internet trolls seems to me an evasion. And, if you come here and claim you are starting an independent wine journal, really you are directly asking for the Gregs (and the me’s) of the world to respond.

Good for you.

Absolutely right, and I am very glad to have engaged your attention.

For the role you envision we have a member within our investors who in fact is exactly that for internal purposes. But no, we will not be adding to the employment roster. The overtime would kill us.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
I genuinely appreciate your skill with obfuscation, elision and implication

Thanks Greg. I aim to please. In return, I appreciate your skill at not being able to recognize answers. You seem to have taken a masterclass. As per usual at this point, I invite anyone to just go over the posts. The answers are simple, I’ve given them, you do not like them… and repeat.

Johan, you haven’t. Can you please answer a simple question - given the stated aim of independence, how do you maintain it given that you are paid for scores by the Chateaux?

Thanks!

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Over and over I’ve stated that at TWI we will join in dinners and tastings when there is a benefit to be had for finding information, tasting notes, stories, photographs or exposure of our message and communication. We will live a normal life in this aspect and not a a gravy train one. Industry standard, and not one we think is problematic when transparency is adopted. Those worried about this are free to feel so. Should they find us disingenuous that is their right and fair enough. Some have standards differing from ours and that’s ok.

We will not participate in the practice of charging money from producers at events. This is now an industry standard for the major players. Transparency, not so much

We will not sell ultra premium subscriptions be they preview or what have you. Something at the moment only present in a couple of places, but examined closely as an avenue by others who pointedly refuse to take a stand against it. As you may have noticed or not. Transparency certainly not applied.

Johan, these aren’t answers, these are vague statements that don’t actually address specific questions. You say you will attend dinners but will not live a “gravy train” life. What does this even mean? Where is gravy train defined? It’s not a “line in the sand” when you’re the one defining what it means!

Had you launched your publication without grand claims of independence, I’d be a lot less interested in these potential conflicts. But, here we are. As I’ve said, you want to have your Latour and drink it too. Also, you are not, of course, required to respond to me here. But you are clearly using this site to advertise your publication, so I’m afraid with publicity comes scrutiny.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: Well, if the dinners you say you will attend, will be paid for by sponsoring chateaux or wines, then this is a practice that Greg has explicitly objected to (and, if it matters, I also find to be a clear conflict of interest). That, of course, is hardly the same as the chateau paying you for a review. But, following normal disclaimer practices, which create the transparency you claim will protect against the conflicts, it does mean that you would, in each review of a chateau or wine that had given such a dinner that you attended, note the fact. You seem to say that you will not do this. I would think that it would be easier, and not particularly destructive to reviewing practices either not to attend such dinners or to insist on paying your way.

Jonathan, just to clarify - it’s not that I explicitly object to attending dinners paid for by the Chateaux, I just want to understand what TWI plans to do. At present, all I can tell is that Johan intends to enjoy life, not be a hermit, and have dinner at chateaux whenever he feels its good for the reader. That appears to me to be no bright line and presents a conflict - I welcome Johan’s answer as to how he intends to mitigate it.

Also, this is, of course, different than paying for a review, but the model Johan is pointing to is the following:

  1. All wines are subject to review.
  2. In order to use scores, Chateaux must pay TWI.

This seems like an inherent conflict of interest to me - though perhaps I’m missing something. I welcome the explanation of why there’s no clear incentive to give higher scores to chateaux paying for them.

Why are people asking skeptical but reasonable questions in this thread “trolls”, while the person relentlessly flogging a commercial product that he is personally heavily invested in isn’t? I mean, I appreciate Johan answering questions here, but his incentive is clear

Troll is a label and labels can be dangerous because we’ve already got in our mind what that term means (and that may vary between us). To be fair the word spam is similar.

I applaud Johan for continuing to politely respond here, to what I think are mostly (but perhaps not entirely) reasonable challenges.
I do encourage him to not use this thread or forum as a marketing vehicle to advertise their latest features / offers. Unsolicited advertising is generally not popular on chat forums.

I agree, but would like to add that continued questioning results in continued responses which might be interpreted as marketing, or maybe not depending upon one’s perspective. Rinse and repeat?

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
The difference between attending a private dinner and attending a dinner sponsored by a chateau and reviewing the wines at either is so gigantic in terms of this issue as to be not in need of further comment.

The disclosure I was considering is one like the Washington Post now runs for every story I read that has Amazon in it: Disclosure, the Washington Post is owned by Jeff Bezos, the owner of Amazon, more or less. So, in your case, Disclosure, the following vertical of wines from Chateau La Luxe Trop Grosse occurred at a dinner sponsored and paid for by Chateau La Luxe Trop Grosse. Not, “I tasted these wines at the Chateau,” by the way. And this would be the minimum. To meet The Post’s standard, every time you reviewed wines from La Luxe Trop Grosse you would print a version of the disclosure saying that you accept dinner and hospitality from the chateau. Since this would add one short line to each such review, it really would not be impossible. Yes, I am aware that it is not the industry standard. I am saying, to the extent that you accept the standard, you are not free of conflict of interest and you are not engaging in full transparency. You are right about one thing: whether that makes your publication’s title The Wine Independent inaccurate, is one your readers will decide. Whether it makes your claim of that independence here accurate is one of readers of the claim here to decide. I am willing to live with that jury.

posts.name: P eter L owet
posts.cooked:
My eyes hurt. It seems pretty simple. Each of us can make a subscription decision based on how one views the editorial contributors and also whether their operating model brings any “independent” value.

Is this an accurate recap of TWI’s stance?

  • we (TWI) are fine with most industry practices so will do the same thing (and disclose in the same way) with respect to tastings, winery-hosted events/dinners, etc. We believe it’s not a conflict of interest because it’s so commonly done, and you’ll like the content that derives from these activities.
  • we will charge a monthly commercial rate to use scores, but are more independent because we will not offer a 2nd special commercial rate for early access
  • we may invite wineries to present their wines at our events, but we won’t charge them for the privilege. (And other publications do charge but don’t tell their readers.)

And one more inference from TWI’s marketing:

  • LPB may have done some stuff at TWA when she was in charge that doesn’t meet TWI standards, but she hated doing it.

Thanks Bruce,

Happy to see another G join. Just hoping the more famous Kenny will also jump in.

To your second point.
This would be of little use I’m afraid. Something at the moment only present in a couple of places, but examined closely as an avenue by others who pointedly refuse to take a stand against it. As you may have noticed or not. Transparency certainly not applied.

To use a favourite expression of another G in the thread, that is our line in the sand. Consumers can have their choice on wether it is drawn correctly. Consumers can decide on if it makes a difference for them or not. Consumers can put their trust where they see fit. Face value.

I do hope Kenny will be a subscriber! After, all I’ve got a signed saxophone of his hanging over the fireplace.

Johan:

Thank you for the reply.
I think we’re heading into a place of diminishing returns on this thread, but just to complete the circle…
Your position strikes me as one that is both overly confident of your ability to sufficiently cover ethical concerns in a single, static statement, and overly dismissive of legitimate concerns of some wine lovers.
This is, ironically, pretty much the attitude adopted by Mr. Parker, with very poor results.

No great loss for you, as I’m not your target market. I don’t really feel the need to pay for wine buying advice.
From my perspective I would love to see wine critiquing transformed into a more responsible, informative and informed discipline. Some day, perhaps.

Regards,

posts.cooked:
My eyes hurt. It seems pretty simple. Each of us can make a subscription decision based on how one views the editorial contributors and also whether their operating model brings any “independent” value.

Is this an accurate recap of TWI’s stance?

  • we (TWI) are fine with most industry practices so will do the same thing (and disclose in the same way) with respect to tastings, winery-hosted events/dinners, etc. We believe it’s not a conflict of interest because it’s so commonly done, and you’ll like the content that derives from these activities.
  • we will charge a monthly commercial rate to use scores, but are more independent because we will not offer a 2nd special commercial rate for early access
  • we may invite wineries to present their wines at our events, but we won’t charge them for the privilege. (And other publications do charge but don’t tell their readers.)

And one more inference from TWI’s marketing:

  • LPB may have done some stuff at TWA when she was in charge that doesn’t meet TWI standards, but she hated doing it.

Head on nail more or less. Or eyes on nails as this case may be. Except Lisa always stayed true to her convictions for what she was able to control and did not have to compromise on that front.

How it started:

“Unbiased wine criticism is important for wine lovers who are seeking to navigate the world of fine wine,” said Perrotti-Brown. “At its foundation, a reputable wine criticism publication should not accept money from any other wine related entities. Many consumers believe this is the case when they read reviews from well-known critics. Still, numerous conflicts of interest have come to light in recent years, such as selling event tables to wineries and score previews to retailers through ultra-premium subscriptions. Some supposedly reputable publications – ones that claim to be independent – are guilty of such practices. As a result, faith in wine reviews has been eroded to the point where buyers no longer know who they can trust.”

How it’s going:

  • We will accept free wine, food, and hospitality from wineries and companies that whose product we review, regardless of cost
  • we will charge a monthly commercial rate to use scores, but we won’t offer a 2nd tier of access
  • we may invite wineries to present their wines at our events, but we won’t charge them for the privilege

posts.name: Barry P
posts.cooked:
Don’t many of these publications start as genuine hobbies, with a stated mission of ethics and independence? Parker started that way in his earliest iterations of TWA; same with Antonio and the Piedmont Report; the Connoisseur’s Guide to Cal Wine (and their puffs); Tanzer’s IWC; the list goes on. Eventually, however, the bills need to get paid and owners want to cash in. So many (though not all) folks sell out, in ways small and large. We’ve all seen that movie, over and over.

But I think the negative reaction from certain quarters of this Board on this latest venture stems from LPB herself. She didn’t start with a hobby and a small publication and a promise of independence. Whatever the full truth is about Singapore investors, editorial control, conflicts of interest, TWA ownership transfer, etc., what seems clear is that, while TWA was already well into its downward spiral, this last failed LPB-led effort at TWA seems to have left many questioning the publication’s and owners’ conflicts. So I think there is a little natural suspicion when LPB comes along and says that she is now going to be the poster child for ethics and how to do things right in the wine reviewing world. It feels to me that the board’s sentiment, kind of comes down to that.

posts.cooked:
How it started:

“Unbiased wine criticism is important for wine lovers who are seeking to navigate the world of fine wine,” said Perrotti-Brown. “At its foundation, a reputable wine criticism publication should not accept money from any other wine related entities. Many consumers believe this is the case when they read reviews from well-known critics. Still, numerous conflicts of interest have come to light in recent years, such as selling event tables to wineries and score previews to retailers through ultra-premium subscriptions. Some supposedly reputable publications – ones that claim to be independent – are guilty of such practices. As a result, faith in wine reviews has been eroded to the point where buyers no longer know who they can trust.”

How it’s going:

  • We will accept free wine, food, and hospitality from wineries and companies that whose product we review, regardless of cost
  • we will charge a monthly commercial rate to use scores, but we won’t offer a 2nd tier of access
  • we may invite wineries to present their wines at our events, but we won’t charge them for the privilege

Welcome back and thank you for the contribution to the circular discussion of repeating statements Matt.

Yup, we will produce stories and reviews on wine. Yup, we will do so using the practices we’ve outlined many a time. Yup, we see them as acceptable and not an issue. And yup, the practices we do take issue with we will stay clear of.

So yeah, since we’re doing exactly what we’ve set out to do and avoiding what Lisa outlines above, it’s going great thanks.

Well, you seem to have perfected the politician’s pivot. Do you mean to contradict any of Matt’s assertions directly?

What do you mean, I agreed with him entirely… He stated our case exactly as Lisa put it forward and repeated exactly what I’ve been saying in this thread and none of it contradicts the other in our opinion as they speak of entirely different issues… and you call that a pivot… to agree? I think you should make use of the OED you mentioned earlier maybe.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
Don’t many of these publications start as genuine hobbies, with a stated mission of ethics and independence? Parker started that way in his earliest iterations of TWA; same with Antonio and the Piedmont Report; the Connoisseur’s Guide to Cal Wine (and their puffs); Tanzer’s IWC; the list goes on. Eventually, however, the bills need to get paid and owners want to cash in. So many (though not all) folks sell out, in ways small and large. We’ve all seen that movie, over and over.

But I think the negative reaction from certain quarters of this Board on this latest venture stems from LPB herself. She didn’t start with a hobby and a small publication and a promise of independence. Whatever the full truth is about Singapore investors, editorial control, conflicts of interest, TWA ownership transfer, etc., what seems clear is that, while TWA was already well into its downward spiral, this last failed LPB-led effort at TWA seems to have left many questioning the publication’s and owners’ conflicts. So I think there is a little natural suspicion when LPB comes along and says that she is now going to be the poster child for ethics and how to do things right in the wine reviewing world. It feels to me that the board’s sentiment, kind of comes down to that.

Hi Barry,

I think the suspicion level is higher than a little from many participants in here. Always has been and it’s just something we accept. On this board there’s a healthy dose of wanting to see the proof in the pudding as it were. And I agree, it’s perfectly natural.

I think some have come to realize, or at least entertain the notion, that all their frustrations which they pinned solely on one person, due to a lack of transparency, may well have been misplaced.

We look forward to making our own way such as it is. And, it did start as just a small hobby for both of us once upon a time.

If that is so, you could merely have answered yes. Rephrasing, however, to make it sound better, does not count. And, by the way, although you have implied assent to these descriptions in responses above, more or less, in watered down languages, you keep avoiding simply saying it. Unless I know what you refer to as “practices we’ve outlined many a time,” which I can’t as it’s a classically vague reference, I can’t know that it addresses what I mention. Since you now say that it addresses (by which I take you to mean assents to) everything I mention, though, I now withdraw the charge. I think Matt’s summation should be taken as the end of the interchange, though.

posts.name: P eter L owet
posts.cooked:
Barry P:

…But I think the negative reaction from certain quarters of this Board on this latest venture stems from LPB herself. She didn’t start with a hobby and a small publication and a promise of independence. Whatever the full truth is about Singapore investors, editorial control, conflicts of interest, TWA ownership transfer, etc., what seems clear is that, while TWA was already well into its downward spiral, this last failed LPB-led effort at TWA seems to have left many questioning the publication’s and owners’ conflicts. So I think there is a little natural suspicion when LPB comes along and says that she is now going to be the poster child for ethics and how to do things right in the wine reviewing world. It feels to me that the board’s sentiment, kind of comes down to that.

Barry,
Thanks for laying out the independence/ethics skepticism so clearly. Many on this board perceived that LPB’s departure from TWA was addition by subtraction. Ironically, given TWI’s marketing, it seems to me that it’s TWA’s image that has gotten a recent upgrade – with the assumption that the editorial staffing changes reflect a pullback from questionable activities. While some people weren’t fans of her reviews/palate, more of the objections revolved around the stuff you mention and she sure seems tarred.

Johan asserts that LPB did not drive the TWA issues and in fact was a reluctant participant (and that it could’ve been even worse if she hadn’t drawn some red lines). [Trying hard not to insert political analogy here!] He and Lisa should realize that credibility is difficult to earn/re-earn. Maybe the skeptics here aren’t the target subscriber market, but maybe they are. One year from now, I’d love to know the number of new TWA subscribers post-LPB vs. the number of new TWI subscribers who followed LPB from TWA.

Without getting into LPB’s virtues (or lack thereof)*, what I’ve found very frustrating are the bald assertions that “we’re comfortable these aren’t conflicts” with respect to clear and obvious conflicts. I’m not suggesting they’re unmanageable conflicts - other publications deal with them too. But there’s a very specific artifice and elision in proclaiming independence because Lisa and Johan just state the conflicts we so obviously see are somehow not conflicts.

It becomes a lot easier to be “independent” when you effectively redefine the word.

*Though I too find it odd to square the “LPB was forced to do bad things at TWA” with the amount of control she supposedly had.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
So, I do not think “accepting free tastings,” as opposed to free bottles, is that objectionable as it’s a necessity to review for en premeur and pre-arrival sales of wines. I know that Parker long ago gave up buying his own wine in wine stores and traveled every year where he would establish himself in hotel rooms and have samples delivered. And, of course, the en primeur Bordeaux tastings each year are sort of a massive event. Even as a rank amateur geek, I have gotten invited to massive professional tastings of Rhone wine. It’s fun for a geek and it’s no doubt one of the more enjoyable ways importers spend their time, but it’s still a way winemakers get their wines tasted.

I don’t think the same is remotely true for hobnobbing with winemakers and accepting their invitations to “event” dinners. I know that it is commonly done. I don’t think a journal that loudly proclaims itself as independent and as eschewing questionable relations with the industry should be doing it. Since Johan has already admitted to doing it, the only thing left is to decide for yourself whether it’s appropriate and, if you care enough, to register that belief here. It’s clear by now what the journal’s practices are and how his answers here will pitch them. I don’t see that he needs further incitement.

Also, for the record, I don’t subscribe to any wine journals or critics web pages, so I am not remotely a likely audience for this journal and Johan probably should not give a tinker’s dam what I think.

Jonathan, I don’t have any issues with any reviewer accepting free samples for review. That’s not something that, to me, constitutes a difficult conflict to mitigate. A sample for review isn’t a good that’s useful for anything other than the review, anyway. I agree with you, accepting invitation to event dinners are an entirely different thing.

I also agree that we have a pretty good sense of Johan’s practices. What I find frustrating is Johan’s continuing attempts to elide them and give misleading answers - suggesting he doesn’t take free things “unless they’re necessary” is a nonsensical response that really should be “yes, we will accept some free things we think are important to do our work”. I do not think I’m alone in that I dislike it when someone is clearly trying to dissemble to make their product look better.

posts.cooked:
• “At its foundation, a reputable wine criticism publication should not accept money from any other wine related entities

  • We will accept free wine, food, and hospitality from wineries and companies that whose product we review, regardless of cost

Am I missing something here?

I guess if someone provides me with a free meal, or a free place to stay and free wine that is different than taking cash?

Yes, you are missing quite a few points in fact. All discussed a few times over by this stage and I doubt many are still confused as to our position on these things.

No, we are not accepting anything free at all when there is not a reason for us to attend or take part as per outlined in the post you quote. No to making a couple of hundred grand from producers at our events, and what have you on premium subscriptions. Yes to producing content in what we consider an acceptable way and are transparent about. You are not required to agree and more than welcome to state that in a post.

Where you get the stay for free thing I don’t know. Preconception is a hard habit to break.

“No, we are not accepting anything free at all…”

“…when there is not a reason for us to attend or take part”

That is so rich.

Could have been richer would you not agree?

posts.name: Bruce G.
posts.cooked:
I think maybe the point is that this free sample hullabaloo was already discussed on the now long forgotten page 2 of this thread already… It was 20K views ago after all.

Johan,

That’s hardly the only aspect of this matter that has been discussed in the interim.

Re: the “free sample hullabaloo”… yes, it has been discussed quite a bit. And, yes, maybe we should all get past it. From the cheap seats, though, the claim “We’re as ethical as we can afford to be” seems like an odd boast no matter how many times one hears it.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked: I heard Larry that Champagne and popcorn can pair well together.

posts.name: M A T T H A R T L E Y
posts.cooked:
Could have been richer would you not agree?

I just don’t know how.

This is as if a travel writer said “I’m starting a new, more ethical / independent travel blog / website - we won’t do any paid posts, and no behind the scenes tricks…”

Then someone says “But will you accept free nights in hotel rooms, free flights in business class to “review” the seats and free meals at restaurants and hotels…”

And the travel writer responds…“But of course, why wouldn’t I? - all I said was we are going to be more ethical and not do ant paid posts where they pay us cash.”
posts.display_username: M A T T H A R T L E Y

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
So, I do not think “accepting free tastings,” as opposed to free bottles, is that objectionable as it’s a necessity to review for en premeur and pre-arrival sales of wines. I know that Parker long ago gave up buying his own wine in wine stores and traveled every year where he would establish himself in hotel rooms and have samples delivered. And, of course, the en primeur Bordeaux tastings each year are sort of a massive event. Even as a rank amateur geek, I have gotten invited to massive professional tastings of Rhone wine. It’s fun for a geek and it’s no doubt one of the more enjoyable ways importers spend their time, but it’s still a way winemakers get their wines tasted.

I don’t think the same is remotely true for hobnobbing with winemakers and accepting their invitations to “event” dinners. I know that it is commonly done. I don’t think a journal that loudly proclaims itself as independent and as eschewing questionable relations with the industry should be doing it. Since Johan has already admitted to doing it, the only thing left is to decide for yourself whether it’s appropriate and, if you care enough, to register that belief here. It’s clear by now what the journal’s practices are and how his answers here will pitch them. I don’t see that he needs further incitement.

Also, for the record, I don’t subscribe to any wine journals or critics web pages, so I am not remotely a likely audience for this journal and Johan probably should not give a tinker’s dam what I think.

Thanks for the back and forth Jonathan and who knows, maybe we’ll meet IRL one day at one of those geek tastings and get along famously. I do actually hope for that. I meet with so many people who I heartily disagree with and you know what, usually we have a lot of fun taking the piss out of each other. A good sport is a good sport.

Cheers,

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
So, I do not think “accepting free tastings,” as opposed to free bottles, is that objectionable is it’s a necessity to review for en premeur and pre-arrival sales of wines. I know that Parker long ago gave up buying his own wine in wine stores and traveled every year where he would establish himself in hotel rooms and have samples delivered. And, of course, the en primeur Bordeaux tastings each year are sort of a massive event. Even as a rank amateur geek, I have gotten invited to massive professional tastings of Rhone wine. It’s fun for a geek and it’s now doubt one of the more enjoyable ways importers spend their time, but it’s still a way winemakers get their wines tasted.

I don’t think the same is remotely true for hobnobbing with winemakers and accepting their invitations to “event” dinners. I know that it is commonly done. I don’t think a journal that loudly proclaims itself as independent and as eschewing questionable relations with the industry should be doing it. Since Johan has already admitted to doing it, the only thing left is to decide for yourself whether it’s appropriate and, if you care enough, to register that belief here. It’s clear by now what the journal’s practices are and how his answers here will pitch them. I don’t see that he needs further incitement.

Jonathan, I don’t have any issues with any reviewer accepting free samples for review. That’s not something that, to me, constitutes a difficult conflict to mitigate. A sample for review isn’t a good that’s useful for anything other than the review, anyway. I agree with you, accepting invitation to event dinners are an entirely different thing.

I also agree that we have a pretty good sense of Johan’s practices. What I find frustrating is Johan’s continuing attempts to elide them and give misleading answers - suggesting he doesn’t take free things “unless they’re necessary” is a nonsensical response that really should be “yes, we will accept some free things we think are important to do our work”. I do not think I’m alone in that I dislike it when someone is clearly trying to dissemble to make their product look better.

You’re not alone.

And yes, your frustration is quite palpable.

While I understand the preference that a critic pays his own way and buys samples and older wines, it is completely unrealistic. It limits any meaningful articles on older wines to writers with a large trust fund. For example, if you wanted to do a vertical of a Second growth Bordeaux like Ducru Beaucaillou, you would end up shelling out several thousand dollars. And if you want to do a vertical of Rousseau you would have to cough up a year’s salary. As for younger wines, it is ridiculous to suggest that you pay for samples. If you attend the UGC tastings in NY, should you also pay towards the airfare, hotels, restaurants, and time of the winemakers attending?

You are either going to limit your coverage or you are going to limit the type of critic. Neither works for me.

posts.name: Barry P
posts.cooked:
In my view, unless wines are tasted blind, in finished bottle, physically away from the influence of a domaine, wine industry representatives, and other tasters, results are bound to be skewed in ways large and small, purposeful and accidental. For example:

• How does a reviewer award a fair score when the puppy dog eyes of the domaine’s owner or wine maker are staring you down with baited breath? Do poor scores get the reviewer invited back?

• How does a reviewer award a fair score when other tasters are in the room? Haven’t we all, at some point, seen how someone’s views morph as a result from group think…once talk turns to how a wine is off or perfect?

• How does a reviewer award a fair score when that reviewer has developed a friendship with an owner or winemaker?

• Is a reviewer’s critique subtly influenced by the winery itself? Whether a new Napa Valley, meticulous and spotless, $25 million gravity flow operation with in-mountain aging caves, or a French primordial and subterranean, wet limestone wonderland.

• How does the reviewer award a fair score when that friendship leads the reviewer to invite his “friend” to serve double magnums of ancient wine at the reviewer’s side hustle?

• How does a reviewer award a fair score when that reviewer is allowed to purchase a case or two of highly allocated wine at cellar door prices?

All to say, there are slimy elements to this business and doing it in a manner unassailable is expensive. That expense has led some to seek remuneration in other ways, e.g., hosting dinners, charity event auctions and educational fora. This, in turn, seems to lead to further potential conflicts. But there are a few who seem to succeed in navigating the pitfalls; others, not so much.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
In my view, unless wines are tasted blind, in finished bottle, physically away from the influence of a domaine, wine industry representatives, and other tasters, results are bound to be skewed in ways large and small, purposeful and accidental. For example:

• How does a reviewer award a fair score when the puppy dog eyes of the domaine’s owner or wine maker are staring you down with baited breath? Do poor scores get the reviewer invited back?

• How does a reviewer award a fair score when other tasters are in the room? Haven’t we all, at some point, seen how someone’s views morph as a result from group think…once talk turns to how a wine is off or perfect?

• How does a reviewer award a fair score when that reviewer has developed a friendship with an owner or winemaker?

• Is a reviewer’s critique subtly influenced by the winery itself? Whether a new Napa Valley, meticulous and spotless, $25 million gravity flow operation with in-mountain aging caves, or a French primordial and subterranean, wet limestone wonderland.

• How does the reviewer award a fair score when that friendship leads the reviewer to invite his “friend” to serve double magnums of ancient wine at the reviewer’s side hustle?

• How does a reviewer award a fair score when that reviewer is allowed to purchase a case or two of highly allocated wine at cellar door prices?

All to say, there are slimy elements to this business and doing it in a manner unassailable is expensive. That expense has led some to seek remuneration in other ways, e.g., hosting dinners, charity event auctions and educational fora. This, in turn, seems to lead to further potential conflicts. But there are a few who seem to succeed in navigating the pitfalls; others, not so much.

You must end up trusting palate which from experience most aligns with yours. If the reviewer is constantly compromised, then the results will show.

But if you really want to recognize the pitfalls, let me give you a few more. Mostly for Futures.

\

  1. The sample you have is not representative. Owner has put aside the best barrels for primeur.

  2. The effects of high or low pressure, which particularly effect young wines.

  3. The timing of the fermentation cycle. For instance soon after malolactic are you tasting?

  4. How soon after breakfast.

  5. How many samples have you tasted that day? You cannot pretend that your palate is the same first thing in the morning or last thing at night.

  6. How old are the samples? (Sampling BTW has much improved from when I went to Bordeaux).

Etc etc.

  3. The timing of the fermentation cycle. For instance soon after malolactic are you tasting?

  6. How old are the samples? (Sampling BTW has much improved from when I went to Bordeaux).

Etc etc.

The standard argument that conflict of interest doesn’t matter is that one can trust the honesty of the person who has the conflict. But that’s not what conflict of interest means. The point is not whether that person will be dishonest but that he or she should avoid situations that tempt such a response. It’s OK if you don’t care about that and wish to trust in the honesty of the reviewer. But, again, this is a journal that sells itself as independent and eschewing certain compromising positions that it thinks other journals have. If it called itself the Wine Insider, the case might be different.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Jonathan, I don’t have any issues with any reviewer accepting free samples for review. That’s not something that, to me, constitutes a difficult conflict to mitigate. A sample for review isn’t a good that’s useful for anything other than the review, anyway. I agree with you, accepting invitation to event dinners are an entirely different thing.

I also agree that we have a pretty good sense of Johan’s practices. What I find frustrating is Johan’s continuing attempts to elide them and give misleading answers - suggesting he doesn’t take free things “unless they’re necessary” is a nonsensical response that really should be “yes, we will accept some free things we think are important to do our work”. I do not think I’m alone in that I dislike it when someone is clearly trying to dissemble to make their product look better.

You’re not alone.

And yes, your frustration is quite palpable.

While I understand the preference that a critic pays his own way and buys samples and older wines, it is completely unrealistic. It limits any meaningful articles on older wines to writers with a large trust fund. For example, if you wanted to do a vertical of a Second growth Bordeaux like Ducru Beaucaillou, you would end up shelling out several thousand dollars. And if you want to do a vertical of Rousseau you would have to cough up a year’s salary. As for younger wines, it is ridiculous to suggest that you pay for samples. If you attend the UGC tastings in NY, should you also pay towards the airfare, hotels, restaurants, and time of the winemakers attending?

You are either going to limit your coverage or you are going to limit the type of critic. Neither works for me.

Mark, I think there are a few distinctions here. First, other than a few absolutists, I doubt 99.99% of people object to “free samples” - these aren’t really worth anything outside of the review process anyway. This isn’t meaningful comp - who gets into wine criticism to get free barrel samples? Especially of Bordeaux!

The free dinners…here things get quite a bit more interesting. First, I don’t really agree that there’s no way to do it without a trust fund - you can do it with investors. Which is what TWI has! And, of course, you can mitigate the conflicts of interest by being very clear about what you’re doing, what events you attend and why. Second, the more events you attend the more you are subject to conflicts - thus my original set of questions. I’m sympathetic to the idea that you can’t review any 1870s Lafite without the Chateau’s input, but I’d question how much you really need to review a wine like that. There needs to be a limit, and here that limit is very unclear. Third, to go back to the investor point, if you set up a publication called The Wine Independent and bring on many investors, what is their role? If their role isn’t to offer readers a publication that maintains independence from the wine industry, how do they expect to see a return? By licensing scores? That adds another layer of conflicts.

Finally, of course, I think what quote a few people are reacting to is not that TWI won’t be fully independent, but rather the attempts to suggest that they are. Johan has basically stated that they will not accept free things “other than what is necessary”. That is quite a bizarre construction - imagine if Johan said that he does not steal - other than things he really needs. That’s quite a perspective!

  6. How old are the samples? (Sampling BTW has much improved from when I went to Bordeaux).

Etc etc.

Mark, normally you make a lot of sense to me. But this post seems off point.

The palate alignment argument is kind of inapposite. That’s sort of like me stating that there is too much traffic on the road, and you saying that I should fly instead. Maybe, but there is still too much traffic on the road, which is what we are discussing.

And your other points seem to implicate the general pitfalls of tasting and aren’t really related to conflicts, which is the central point of this discussion.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Thanks for posting that Greg as it pretty much crystallises my views on hospitality. I’ve added further thoughts on it below.

  • Free samples for tasting purposes is sensible and practical. In terms of declarations, I’d see this as a ‘once and done’ in an organisations ethical declaration, never needing to be updated. If the odd producer sends 2 bottles “in case one is corked”, then that could easily leave the critic with the backup as a saleable bottle. I’ve no idea how often this occurs, but I would say an ethical critic would put that spare bottle aside for retasting as it enters their predicted ‘drinking window’, rather than selling or gifting it. Probably not worth worrying over though, but if sent half a case of the same wine, then that’s something they should firmly and politely refuse (if it ever happened)

  • Where there is an organised tasting, the write-up should declare what hospitality was provided, be that being put up for the night, provided with free food, taxis paid for etc.

  • Ditto for regional visits, and this is where the hospitality can ramp up significantly e.g. being flown out free of charge, with accommodation, food etc. also provided free. Making a habit of declaring is a minimum, but it also requires some common sense e.g. don’t accept a all-expenses paid multi day cruise to taste wines with an importer.

  • If it not necessary, then seek to decline it. If it is, don’t open yourself up to bribery by accepting OTT hospitality. For those that work in most larger companies, there are typically clear and tight guidelines, which IIRC at the last place (for example), said gifts over $30 cannot be accepted. Similar rules were in place for hospitality.

  • If in doubt, declare it visibly and in a consistent manner. People understand the difference between a plate of cheese sandwiches and a luxury hotel stay with michelin starred food, and can reasonably conclude that the host might not expect any favours for the cheese sandwich, but that there must be a justification for that larger spend, with an expectation they will get value from it. If the value expected is ‘assessing our wines in a conducive setting’, then that’s already a concern.


    Beyond hospitality, be very careful to distance scores from payment, so that the conflict of interest (higher scores => more money received) is minimised.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
Ian,

You are somewhat less strict than I would be. All travel should be at the expense of the journal or the critic. One should not accept any hospitality that goes beyond palate clearing snacks at large tastings or, if the organization, doing the hospitality is not being reviewed (Grand tasting of all wines of an area put on by a regional organization and becoming a massive event). Seeking to pay one’s way should be the norm to the extent possible. And, again, all of this becomes more significant if one trumpets one’s own independence. At that point, Caesar’s wife rules start to kick in.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Ian,

You are somewhat less strict than I would be. All travel should be at the expense of the journal or the critic. One should not accept any hospitality that goes beyond palate clearing snacks at large tastings or, if the organization, doing the hospitality is not being reviewed (Grand tasting of all wines of an area put on by a regional organization and becoming a massive event). Seeking to pay one’s way should be the norm to the extent possible. And, again, all of this becomes more significant if one trumpets one’s own independence. At that point, Caesar’s wife rules start to kick in.

Hi Jonathan
It’s ok to diverge on this.

My views are very much beyond this one publication, to include the likes of Chris Kissack, Jeff Leve and the likes of a young Antonio Galloni writing his Piedmont report. If Lisa is independently funded then great, but my thoughts are seeking to strike a balance for those that aren’t, to strike an ethical balance, and at the very least not hide the incriminating evidence from their readers

If trumpeting independence and a higher standard of ethics (as Parker did and Lisa is doing now), then yes we should rightly expect a tighter code of ethics with greater definition than “it’s ok if we think it’s necessary”. With that also a greater transparency than Parker gave, with later revelations showing he’d not adhered to what he’d been claiming.

Regards
Ian

p.s. I used to follow a prominent UK blogger, but stopped doing so not long after he reported on an all expenses paid trip to Australia, tasting the very finest wines Australia had to offer (young and especially old). It was a level of hospitality that crashed well over what I feel is practical to then keep an open mind about reporting back on it. Sometimes even open declaration is not sufficient.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked: The only way to make this thread better would be for Lisa to chime in.

posts.name: Jeff Leve

Seeking to pay one’s way should be the norm to the extent possible. And, again, all of this becomes more significant if one trumpets one’s own independence. At that point, Caesar’s wife rules start to kick in.

Hi Jonathan
It’s ok to diverge on this.

My views are very much beyond this one publication, to include the likes of Chris Kissack, Jeff Leve and the likes of a young Antonio Galloni.

As I was mentioned and as I’ll be in Bordeaux for 3 weeks starting Saturday… This is what I do, and don’t.

I pay for my own ticket, car rental and gas. I have dinners with the same people every year. I stay at friends homes, which could also be a chateau. I attend tastings. Though almost every tasting I go to is 1 on 1.

Roughly, I will make 110 visits over 20 days which includes wineries, negociants and consultants. I think I’ll taste at least 600 wines from 2021 and with luck, a myriad of other vintages from producers I usually spend more time with, which could bring the total number of wines tasted to about 1,000. I never ask to taste older or special bottles, though it does happen which I like.

On the other side of the coin, I think everyone I stay with has been a guest in my home for at least a lunch or dinner and some have also stayed in my home.

Over the years I think most of the people I dine with have also had lunch or dinner in my house.

Until Covid I was never sent samples or bottles. I have never been offered free wines to bring home.

This has no bearing on what others do, or don’t, or should or should not do.

I’m not sure Bordeaux is planning on sending samples now that people can travel. Though, I’m going to request that many of the smaller wines that I’ve been tasting during Covid send samples of they want to be tasted.

posts.name: Michael Mager
posts.cooked:
This thread is a great source of amusement for me. Given all the spinning Johan keeps doing, I get dizzy even watching from the sidelines.

Ultimately, it’s a case of, as The Who said, “… meet the new boss, same as the old boss …” Lisa maintains plausible deniability since Johan is always out front, and then cuts him loose as sacrificial lamb when things eventually go sideways. For now, reading this is just a harmless, guilty pleasure…
