posts.cooked:
Seeking to pay one’s way should be the norm to the extent possible. And, again, all of this becomes more significant if one trumpets one’s own independence. At that point, Caesar’s wife rules start to kick in.

Well, as for “extent possible” I guess we have found agreement as that is exactly what we will be doing. Really.

As for trumpeting. While we do have the name independence in our name we are not trumpeting that claim more than our competitors. Everyone blasts that horn no matter what practices are applied on a business model level.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
The free dinners…here things get quite a bit more interesting. First, I don’t really agree that there’s no way to do it without a trust fund - you can do it with investors. Which is what TWI has! And, of course, you can mitigate the conflicts of interest by being very clear about what you’re doing, what events you attend and why. Second, the more events you attend the more you are subject to conflicts - thus my original set of questions. I’m sympathetic to the idea that you can’t review any 1870s Lafite without the Chateau’s input, but I’d question how much you really need to review a wine like that. There needs to be a limit, and here that limit is very unclear. Third, to go back to the investor point, if you set up a publication called The Wine Independent and bring on many investors, what is their role? If their role isn’t to offer readers a publication that maintains independence from the wine industry, how do they expect to see a return? By licensing scores? That adds another layer of conflicts.

Finally, of course, I think what quote a few people are reacting to is not that TWI won’t be fully independent, but rather the attempts to suggest that they are. Johan has basically stated that they will not accept free things “other than what is necessary”. That is quite a bizarre construction - imagine if Johan said that he does not steal - other than things he really needs. That’s quite a perspective!

You seem to be suffering quite an acute case of hyperbolism Greg. Overcomplicationism also appears to be an underlying factor.

You do in fact need a trust fund in order to do what you suggest. An investor would never see a return in your scenario. I think you understand that. In our scenario, you know, the actual reasonable one, the investors see a return using the model we have outlined. Since you seem to have quite impressive blinders on I shall copy paste once more: “No, we are not accepting anything free at all when there is not a reason for us to attend or take part as per outlined. No to making a couple of hundred grand from producers at our events, and what have you on premium subscriptions. Yes to producing content in what we consider an acceptable way and are transparent about.”

As for the stealing analogy, well that’s just reminds me of this sage advice: https://youtu.be/X6WHBO_Qc-Q



Quite a perspective indeed as you say. Which naturally then leads to this gem: https://youtu.be/nAruBWa7LGk

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Let’s go right back to the beginning of this thread… (with my bolding)

Lisa Perrotti-Brown MW launches ‘fiercely independent’ wine publication

Lots of interesting details here, but this stood out:

… Speaking to db last week ahead of the official launch of the new publication, Perrotti-Brown said that she and [her 50-50 partner Swedish photojournalist Johan Berglund] “had been playing with so many names” for the title, but “it was The Wine Independent that came to us, because it seemed to say exactly what we wanted.”

Continuing, she told db, > “I don’t want to point fingers at anyone else’s business in particular, but people don’t know what’s happening behind the scenes of so many wine criticism publications, unless you’re in the business, and then you are like, ‘oh my God’.”

She added, “> A consumer reading a publication that tells you what you should and shouldn’t buy has no idea there are other agendas behind the scenes, so we are saying we are independent in the name, there is never going to be any wriggle room on that, there is never going to be any compromise on integrity> , and that comes with us [Perotti-Brown and Berglund] being majority shareholders, and the others being very much in agreement with our ethos – no-one is going to get greedy; we want to remain completely unbiased.”

Indeed, in the press release on the title’s launch, which was sent to db last week, the new publication is said to “return to the high ethical standards initially championed by Robert M. Parker, Jr. back when he started The Wine Advocate in 1978,” noting that “a reputable wine criticism publication should not accept money from any other wine-related entities” …

The first bolding does not sit well with this statement

As for trumpeting. While we do have the name independence in our name we are not trumpeting that claim more than our competitors. Everyone blasts that horn no matter what practices are applied on a business model level.

I don’t know of anyone since Parker that broadly smeared the rest of their competitors in this way. That quote from Lisa has IMO set the tone for much of what has followed. Smearing the competition will ensure your own ethical standards will be challenged hard, and Wine Berserkers are not shy to do this, given such challenges to TWA were very much a part of the process that resulted in this site’s creation.


The second bolding, of no compromise on integrity, sets a bar that is not met by accepting hospitality when the critic deems it ok. No compromise is almost certainly an impossible hurdle, but if that’s the claim being made, then again it will be challenged, not least because of what we found out about The Wine Advocate’s ethical claims that we later found out weren’t supported by reality.


I do however want to say that recent posts have become tetchy and some might be viewed as personal attacks. Hard challenges and robust defence are to be expected in such a thread, but please don’t say anything that would stop you sharing a drink in the future with someone who you disagree with.

and this is the classic irony of high profile wine reviewers. Punters pay to follow the wines that have their prices raised by high scores, and are put off wines where a *comparatively low score causes prices to fall. If you believe the hype about professionals leading us inadequate amateurs through the maze of wine, such scores can be reassuring. If you think of the influencers like the Wizard of Oz, mere humans behind the curtain of hype, then the whole charade becomes deeply ironic - paying money to the reviewer, so you can pay more for the wines you buy!

In a different field, there is a subtly different irony of the Rick Steves guide, touted as Italy away from the major tourist sites, but such has been its success (and such is the appetite for sheep-like following of such guides), that many of the places are now blighted by mass tourism. It’s another reminder that making the effort to do our own research and seek what is right for us, produces much better results than following a self-annointed influencer, whose tastes almost certainly don’t align with our own.

\

  • frankly 86/100 is not a low score in the majority of fields, but in wine criticism it’s the equivalent of **Monty Python’s French taunter
    ** At times I feel like Brian in Life of Brian… “you’re all individuals”

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
She’s back!

2019 Dominus
"Composed of 90% Cabernet Sauvignon, 4% Cabernet Franc, and 6% Petit Verdot, the 2019 Dominus was harvested from September 29 to October 10. It was aged in 40% new oak. Deep garnet-purple colored, the nose slowly unfurls to unveil beguiling floral notes of candied violets and rose oil over a core of crème de cassis, blackberry pie, and plum preserves, giving way to notions of licorice, Indian spices, iron ore, and crushed rocks. Full-bodied, the palate is jam-packed with taut, muscular black fruit layers, intertwined with gorgeous floral and exotic spice accents, and framed by firm, finely grained tannins with seamless freshness, finishing long and mineral laced. > Tightly coiled > with so much latent energy waiting to explode, this is a spectacular expression of the vintage and Napanook vineyard. > Still tightly coiled> , give it a good 6-7 years in the cellar before broaching, and allow it a few hours in a decanter if consumed before 2032. 3500 cases made, to be released in May 2022. > - Lisa Perotti-Brown, The Wine Independent

Notes:

  1. Doubly coiled!
  2. Wouldn’t it be refreshing if a critic setting out on their own began by being very critical of a well-regarded wine, as Parker was with Bordeaux in the early 80s? Now it seems like a race to the top in points/bottom in terms of critical appraisal.

FYI

https://twitter.com/Livex/status/1646116658916761601?s=20

Here’s LPB’s pitch to the trade on Facebook, which seems to translate to: “You can snap up supplies and raise prices before consumers see the scores.”

“Our commercial subscribers who are members of Liv-ex will have instant access to our reviews as soon as they are published, which will help them to make fast buying decisions. If we’re recommending something you have in stock, our reviews are at your fingertips to help you promote it!”

It’s been pretty clear for a number of years that the business model for most wine critics has relied on revenue from the trade, rather than consumer subscriptions. You can draw your own conclusions about their reviews and scores.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
Since I have never read a word of your journal, I have no basis to like or dislike it. I am responding only to what you say here. Let’s go over the history:

  1. John quotes a Facebook ad promising commercial subscribers the ability to make faster buying decisions. You say he has misread that (he didn’t) and that everybody has the same advantage.

2)I say, if that’s the case, what precisely are you offering commercial subscribers IN THAT AD. You answer that you are offering the advantage of having all your information in one place. I answer that that’s absurd, not because it’s not a value, but because it’s not a value specific to commercial subscriber, or indeed almost any subscriber to an online journal. I get the same value even with my Amazon subscription to the NYRB. Nothing you say in this post is responsive to that or to the original discussion of what you said in Facebook. Indeed, your remark about having access to everything in one place also has nothing to do with that interchange.

I note, by the way, that Andy SC’s response is specific and accurate with regard to the advantage of a commercial subscription as opposed to an individual one. You might have tried saying that. It would at least have been responsive to a general question of added value for commercial subscribers, though it still wouldn’t have been responsive to what John said.

I agree. If the trade subscribers don’t get scores ahead of consumers, it’s not such a blatant conflict.

But the pitch in the press release – and the big premium charged for trade subscriptions – shows who really values the scores, and where the revenue will be coming from.

Here’s some back-of-the-envelope math: In NY state, there are 3,700 wine and liquor stores. Now, most of those won’t subscribe. But you can bet that hundreds will. Add in all the importers and distributors, and I bet there will be more trade than consumer subscribers in the state.

Too much wine criticism these days just comes down to promotion and shelf talkers. I’m sure some of TWI’s puffy features will appeal to some consumers, but I haven’t seen any critical content that would be uniquely valuable to consumers but unpopular with the trade. That’s the real test of whether it’s a consumer publication.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: Thanks. I won’t have much time in the next week, but I’ll take advantage of that when I know I’ll have some reading time.

It’s been pretty clear for a number of years that the business model for most wine critics has relied on revenue from the trade, rather than consumer subscriptions

That is absolutely true. I didn’t know many individuals who ever had subscriptions to WA when Parker was writing, but every store had a copy. Importers and distributors got calls when the issue would come out.

If consumers and trade get their info at the same time, which is generally what happens with the NYT and the SF Chronicle, those consumers who are anxious to buy will be happy. But they buy a few bottles or cases. Back in the day, the distributors calling for the WA scores wanted many cases because then they could take things to their accounts and say they have that 93 point wine for $15.

But that was Parker. NYT used to move a bit of wine initially, but their reviews didn’t have any legs. Two weeks after publication, nobody cared. And I don’t think even the NYT has the kind of clout they used to any more. There are so many critics out there now that it’s all pretty irrelevant. A high score matters for a shelf-talker mostly, and if you look a bit, you’ll probably find one for pretty much any wine.

In any event, here’s a suggested marketing plan for whatever it’s worth - sell discounted subscriptions next Berserker Day. Just make sure that the reviews don’t have references to notes of cast iron pans or they’ll end up on the bingo thread.

posts.name: Marc Hauser
posts.cooked:
GregT:

I didn’t know many individuals who ever had subscriptions to WA when Parker was writing, but every store had a copy. Importers and distributors got calls when the issue would come out.

I admit that I have just about as little actual data as you do about this, but I suspect your assertion that most of the subscribers were trade is incorrect. Per Wikipedia, first, Parker started writing WA as a subscription service in 1984, so you’re talking about a very long period of time during which “Parker was writing”. Also, apparently there were 50,000 subscribers by 2012 - I can’t imagine that the majority of those are trade. Finally, this is sort of ancillary to the point, but he sold a lot of books that had the same rating system, and I can’t imagine those were mostly bought by trade.

Personally, I learned about WA from a friend and fellow wine nerd in the early aughts - he was a non-trade subscriber, and I became one as well. So now you know of at least one more individual who had a subscription when Parker was writing.

I don’t disagree with some of the broader premise that the trade is dependent upon ratings to move wine (although that happens with plenty of other products with a large critic base, from movies to books to art to fashion), but I suspect that WA was enjoyed by more individual wine lovers/nerds than you suggest.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
ToddFrench:

Easy fix, outlined here

Yes, David directed me there also. And yeah as I said, I was about to join the group but noticed that not a single one my fellow compatriots from TWA, Decanter, Vinous and what have you had done so. And I guess have not been asked to. I will happily follow suit.

Cheers

Different strokes I guess. None of my serious wine friends had subscriptions. They didn’t particularly care. The people who subscribed in the 1990s and 2000s were people who wanted to buy highly-scored wines.

When Parker started, wine wasn’t a big deal in the US, so most of his subscribers were learning along with him. When he had become the established voice, sometime in the 1990s, retailers had learned that shelf talkers were effective sales tools. You could walk into the most forgotten dusty wine store in Manhattan or Brooklyn or the Bronx and they would have the WA laying around. When he got enough subscriptions to quit his job as a lawyer, those weren’t broken out into individuals vs trade and the early subscribers may well have been individuals. But by the 1990s, anyone in the trade knew that the retailers would start putting in orders as soon as the latest edition came out. The WA wasn’t particularly interesting to read.

We worked through all available systems to find the best fit, and are quite pleased with this system, allowing us a better user experience, FAR easier media sharing, and customizable interfaces, in color combinations, font size, even overall format (list versus category view,…

link_counts.title: Welcome to our NEW Home! (some 'how to' guidance here)

posts.name: Ray Ormand
posts.cooked:
I took advantage of the six day trial subscription. I enjoyed it, but unsubscribed–and deleted the bookmark bar reference–after two days. Imagine my surprise when, four days later, I was charged $140 for a year’s subscription. Immediately contacted customer service re my unsubscribe request. No response; so there’s that.

On the other hand, I found it to be a first class publication with exceptional photography and an interesting, reflective approach to evaluating wine.

I unsubscribed only because 90% of my wine buying is Napa/Sonoma.

I wish them well, but would be happy if my unsubscribe request was honored.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
Hi Ray,

Sorry to hear that something went amiss there. We have had quite a nice rate of people staying on with the free trial subscription offer. And then again, some have opted out also. It has not been an issue previously that this has happened with the system not registering cancel request. An opt out is an opt out.

I actually went through my incoming emails forwarded to me last night and saw yours. Did not have time to respond but it will be amended this weekend. It’s a fairly full on candy crush en primeur schedule so let’s blame the Bordelaise for my tardiness.

We are make a huge Napa/Sonoma push this year so I hope to pied piper you back…

Cheers,
Johan

posts.name: Ray Ormand
posts.cooked: Thank you Johan.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked: Please answer our questions in the relevant thread johan. Thanks!

I don’t have or didn’t realise I had any grief. I like the Wine Independent and I like and encourage what it espouses and promotes. I have no opinion on the Wine Independent taking a particular position (implied or otherwise) on other wine publications and their lack of independence. That is all by the by.

But if it is true that a wine receives a different score, in-bottle, tasted blind, than the same wine, in-bottle, tasted non-blind, and that there is a certain bias to be discovered somewhere to the bigger chateaux and marques especially with regard to such scores, then that is a serious problem, and whether or not that is an issue across the industry or not, it puts the independent notion of the Wine Independent up for debate. Maybe that is my naivety - I can imagine that a truly independent publication would get blacklisted or otherwise be out of favour quite regularly - but then that needs to be caveated.

Maybe I should just be grateful these are not issues I have to contend with on a day to day…
